Wash it down with a glass of Ribera del Duero or a vermut de grifo (vermouth on tap), Madrid's traditional aperitif drink.

practical_info.tip: The bars along Calle de las Huertas and around Plaza de Santa Ana have the best addresses

visits.text: Devote the morning to the Royal Palace, official residence of the Spanish monarchs—though the royal family no longer lives there. Built between 1738 and 1764 on the site of the former Habsburg Alcázar destroyed by fire, it's the largest palace in Western Europe by floor area: 135,000 m² and 3,418 rooms. You'll visit the state rooms—the Throne Room with its Tiepolo frescoes, the Gala Dining Room with its 140-seat table, and the Royal Armoury, one of the world's most complete collections with armour belonging to Charles V and Philip II. Allow 1.5 to 2 hours.

Leaving the palace, cross Plaza de la Armería to the Almudena Cathedral. Begun in 1883, it wasn't completed and consecrated until 1993 by Pope John Paul II—over a century of construction. The interior is surprisingly modern, with brightly painted ceilings that contrast sharply with the sobriety of the neoclassical façade. Climb to the dome for views over the palace and Casa de Campo.

Walk down to Plaza Mayor via Calle Mayor. This arcaded rectangular square, completed in 1619 under Philip III, was the scene of Inquisition autos-da-fé, bullfights, and public executions. Today, the terraces beneath the porticos are tourist traps—just admire the architecture and the equestrian statue of Philip III at the centre, then head to Mercado de San Miguel, just next door. This covered market from 1916, with its iron-and-glass structure, has become a gourmet temple: oysters, jamón ibérico, churros, vermouth, and creative tapas.

Head up to Gran Vía, Madrid's main commercial artery, cut through between 1910 and 1929. The Edificio Metrópolis (1911), at the corner of Calle de Alcalá, with its gilded dome and winged Victory, is the city's postcard image. Continue west to the Templo de Debod, an authentic Egyptian temple from the 2nd century BC, gifted by Egypt to Spain in 1968 in thanks for its help saving the Nubian temples threatened by the Aswan Dam. At sunset, the temple's reflections in the pool with Casa de Campo in the background offer one of Madrid's most beautiful views.

activities.text: If you're in Madrid on a Sunday morning, don't miss El Rastro, Spain's largest flea market. It spreads out every Sunday from 9am to 3pm around Calle de la Ribera de Curtidores, in the La Latina neighbourhood. Antiques, vintage clothing, books, vinyl, curiosities—it's a joyful bazaar that attracts Madrileños as much as visitors. After the market, the tapas bars of La Latina are mobbed—it's Madrid's Sunday ritual.

practical_info.tip: El Rastro only happens on Sundays. The rest of the week, La Latina neighbourhood remains pleasant for tapas

activities.text: In the evening, catch a flamenco show. Madrid isn't Andalusia, but the city has a historic flamenco scene—many Andalusian artists have settled here since the 19th century. Tablaos in the La Latina and Lavapiés neighbourhoods offer intimate performances with singing, guitar, and dance. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours for a show.

practical_info.tip: Book in advance, especially on weekends—the tablaos are small and fill up quickly

visits.text: Two hours' drive south from Madrid and you enter the heart of La Mancha—Don Quixote's plains. Almagro, declared a Historic-Artistic Ensemble, is a complete surprise in this landscape of windmills and fields. The Plaza Mayor is its jewel: a rectangular square bordered by two stone-arcaded galleries with 81 columns, and characteristic green-painted glazed balconies—a legacy of the German Fugger bankers (los Fúcares in Spanish) who settled here in the 16th century to manage the Almadén mercury mines on behalf of Charles V.

The Corral de Comedias is the reason for your visit to Almagro. Built in 1628 by Leonardo de Oviedo in the former Mesón del Toro, it's the only Spanish Golden Age theatre preserved intact and still active in the world. Its 300 m² rectangular courtyard, its two-storey wooden galleries, its cazuela (balcony reserved for women), and its tablado (stage) transport you directly to the era of Lope de Vega, Calderón de la Barca, and Tirso de Molina. Closed in the 18th century by royal decree, it was rediscovered by chance in 1954 beneath the partitions of a boarding house. Every summer, the International Festival of Classical Theatre revives Golden Age plays there.

The Museo Nacional del Teatro, housed in the 13th-century Maestral Palace of the Knights of Calatrava, is Spain's only national museum devoted to theatre. It traces the history of performing arts from the Middle Ages: set models, period costumes, manuscripts, puppets, and posters. An entire section is dedicated to the corrales de comedias and theatrical life during the Golden Age.

The Almacén de los Fúcares—the Fuggers' warehouse—is an imposing 16th-century building that served as a grain store and mercury warehouse for the Augsburg bankers. These financiers were so powerful they lent money to Charles V in exchange for the Almadén mercury monopoly. The building, sometimes called a palace because its construction is so refined, now hosts exhibitions. Just next door, the Church of San Agustín, declared of Cultural Interest, deserves a look for its Renaissance cloister.

activities.text: The gastronomy of Almagro and the Campo de Calatrava belongs to the heart of La Mancha. Try the berenjenas de Almagro—small round aubergines pickled in vinegar with cumin and chilli, a local speciality with its own protected designation. Add to that migas manchegas (fried breadcrumbs with chorizo), pisto manchego (a local ratatouille), and a glass of Valdepeñas wine from the neighbouring appellation.

practical_info.tip: If you're in Almagro in July, the International Festival of Classical Theatre transforms the town for a month

map_keywords: Castillo de Burgalimar Baños de la Encina
Iglesia San Mateo Baños de la Encina

visits.text: The road south from Almagro crosses the Sierra Morena through the Despeñaperros gorge—the natural frontier between Castile and Andalusia. This narrow pass between quartzite cliffs has been the gateway to Andalusia for centuries: Roman armies, Visigoths, Moors, and Christian armies all passed through. Stop at the viewpoint for a view over the gorges, then detour to the Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa Interpretation Centre in Santa Elena. On 16 July 1212, the Christian coalition led by Alfonso VIII of Castile crushed the Almohad army here—a battle that reversed the course of the Reconquest and definitively opened Andalusia to the Christian kingdoms.

Baños de la Encina appears among the olive groves, dominated by the spectacular silhouette of Castillo de Burgalimar. This Caliphate fortress, built in 968 on the orders of Caliph Al-Hakam II of Córdoba, is one of the best-preserved Muslim fortresses in Europe. Its 14 towers and its wall of tapial (rammed earth, lime, and stone) still stand at their original height—a millennium later. A 15th tower, the Keep, was added by the Christians after the reconquest. It's called 'the fortress of seven kings' because seven monarchs passed through, from Alfonso VII to Ferdinand the Catholic. Climb the 18-metre keep: the panorama over the endless sea of olive trees is one of the most striking images in the province of Jaén.

Coming down from the castle, stroll through the streets of Baños de la Encina. Unlike Andalusia's white villages, here the façades are ashlar stone—16th and 17th-century manor houses with carved coats of arms line the streets. The Church of San Mateo (15th century), in late Gothic style, dominates the main square. Don't miss the anthropomorphic tombs carved into the rock near the Molino del Santo, vestiges of a Visigothic past.

activities.text: Baños de la Encina sits at the heart of the world's largest olive grove—the province of Jaén alone produces about 20% of the world's olive oil. Local operators offer visits to olive groves and oil mills with tastings. You'll learn to distinguish the Picual variety (the most planted here, intense and fruity) from Arbequina, and to recognise a 'virgen extra' from a simple 'virgen'. It's an experience that permanently changes your relationship with olive oil.

practical_info.tip: The olive harvest runs from November to February—it's the best time to see the oil mills in action

visits.text: Baeza is one of the two towns inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2003 for their Renaissance ensembles. But before becoming a 16th-century jewel, it was a stronghold of the Reconquest: Ferdinand III conquered it in 1227, and it became Castile's forward base facing the Kingdom of Granada for two centuries. Begin at Plaza del Pópulo, the civic heart of the town. The Fuente de los Leones (Lion Fountain), which resembles the one in the Alhambra, is actually an Ibero-Roman remnant from the ancient city of Cástulo. Around the square, the old slaughterhouse (Antigua Carnicería) and the Villalar Arch complete a harmonious ensemble.

Climb to Plaza de Santa María, the religious and educational heart of Baeza. The Cathedral of the Nativity, rebuilt in the 16th century on a former mosque, blends Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque. Its main façade is sober, but the interior surprises with the brightness of its vaults and the richness of its chapels. Just opposite, the Palacio de Jabalquinto is Baeza's most photographed monument: its Isabelline façade, covered with carved stone diamond points, is a masterpiece of stone lacework from the 15th century. It now houses the headquarters of the International University of Andalusia.

The Old University of Baeza, founded in 1538, is an austere Renaissance building with a beautiful arcaded courtyard. The poet Antonio Machado taught French there between 1912 and 1919—his classroom has remained intact, with his desk, blackboard, and student benches. It was here that he wrote part of his Campos de Castilla. On the wall, a plaque quotes his verses: 'En estos campos de la tierra mía...' Entry is free and the emotion is real.

Finish with a walk along the Paseo de la Constitución and the ramparts, from where the view embraces the olive fields stretching to the horizon. The sunset over this green and silver sea is a daily spectacle that Baeza's inhabitants never tire of watching.

activities.text: Baeza is a town of tapas and olive oil. The bars around Plaza de la Constitución and Calle San Pablo serve generous portions drizzled with Sierra de Cazorla PDO olive oil. Try the ochíos (olive oil bread typical of Jaén), pipirrana (cold salad of tomato, pepper, and cucumber), and revuelto de ajetes (scrambled eggs with tender garlic). Olive oil here isn't a condiment—it's a main ingredient.

practical_info.tip: Baeza and Úbeda are 9 km apart—you can easily dine in one and sleep in the other

visits.text: If Baeza is the ecclesiastical and university town, Úbeda is the noble and palatial one—the other half of the UNESCO inscription. It all begins at Plaza Vázquez de Molina, considered one of Spain's most beautiful Renaissance squares. Francisco de los Cobos y Molina, Secretary of State to Charles V, and his nephew Juan Vázquez de Molina, who succeeded him under Philip II, had an ensemble of palaces and chapels built here that rival anything in Tuscany. The architect Andrés de Vandelvira, a native of the region, designed most of the buildings—his influence would later be found in Latin American cathedrals.

The Sacra Capilla del Salvador, commissioned by Francisco de los Cobos as a private funerary chapel, is Vandelvira's masterpiece in Úbeda. Completed in 1559, its Plateresque façade displays extravagant sculptural richness—mythological scenes, grotesques, atlantes, the entire vocabulary of the Italian Renaissance translated into Andalusian stone. Inside, Vandelvira's sacristy is a jewel of proportion, with its caryatids and atlantes framing the windows. Berruguete's monumental altarpiece was partially destroyed during the Civil War, but what remains is impressive.

Opposite the chapel, the Palacio del Deán Ortega (16th century), now a Parador hotel, deserves a visit even if you're not staying there—go in to see the two-level arcaded Renaissance courtyard and the gallery with views over the olive groves. At the other end of the square, the Palacio de las Cadenas (Palace of the Chains), former palace of Juan Vázquez de Molina, now serves as the town hall. Its façade is a model of Spanish purist Renaissance.

Head down to the old quarter to discover the Sinagoga del Agua, discovered by chance during renovation work in 2007. This underground site comprises a prayer hall, a ritual bath (mikveh), a women's gallery, and a well—the most complete medieval synagogue ensemble found in Spain. The guided tour is fascinating and illuminates the life of Úbeda's Jewish community before the 1492 expulsion.

activities.text: The alfarero quarter (potters' quarter) of San Millán, in the lower part of town, perpetuates a pottery tradition dating back to Moorish times. The workshops produce pieces in emerald green on a white background—Úbeda's typical style. Push open a workshop door to see the artisans at work, and leave with a dish or jar as a souvenir. It's one of the few places in Spain where traditional pottery is still a living craft, not a tourist attraction.

practical_info.tip: The alfarero quarter is at the bottom of town—go down via Calle Valencia. Workshops generally close between 2pm and 5pm

visits.text: The road from Úbeda to Cazorla crosses a sea of olive trees stretching as far as the eye can see—the province of Jaén has over 66 million olive trees. Cazorla is a white village clinging to a hillside at the foot of the natural park. Plaza de Santa María, one of the two main squares, is built around the spectacular ruins of the Iglesia de Santa María—a 16th-century Renaissance church bombed during the Civil War and never rebuilt. Its open-air arches, with the mountain backdrop, create a unique theatrical setting.

The Castillo de la Yedra (Ivy Castle), of Arab origin then enlarged by the Christians, dominates Cazorla from its rocky spur. It houses the Museo de Artes y Costumbres Populares del Alto Guadalquivir—an ethnographic museum tracing rural life in the sierras. From the ramparts, the view plunges over Cazorla's rooftops and the first forests of the natural park. It's also an excellent spot to watch griffon vultures soaring above the gorges.

Two kilometres from Cazorla, the village of La Iruela has one of the most spectacular sites in the province: a castle perched on a rocky pinnacle so narrow it seems to defy gravity. The adjoining ruined church, clinging to the same rock, reinforces the vertiginous impression. The climb is short but steep—the view from the summit embraces the entire Cerezuelo valley and the distant sierras.

activities.text: Cazorla is the ideal starting point for exploring the natural park, but it's also a town where you eat well. The restaurants around Plaza de la Constitución serve game (deer, wild boar), Segureño lamb, seasonal mushrooms, and of course Sierra de Cazorla PDO olive oil on everything. Rin-ran (cod, tomato, and pepper salad) is the local speciality. Dine early—mountain restaurants close earlier than in the city.

practical_info.tip: The car park at the top of the village near the castle is often full—park at the bottom and walk up

visits.text: Full day in the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas Natural Park—at 210,000 hectares, it's Spain's largest protected area and Europe's second largest. Declared a Biosphere Reserve by UNESCO in 1983, it's home to over 2,170 plant species including 34 endemics (Cazorla violet, the world's smallest narcissus, a carnivorous plant). Wildlife includes ibex, deer, wild boar, mouflon, griffon vultures, golden eagles, and the rare bearded vulture.

The Río Borosa hike is the park's flagship trail—and one of Andalusia's most beautiful. From the Torre del Vinagre visitor centre, the path follows the Borosa, the Guadalquivir's first major tributary, for about 10 km one way. You first cross the Cerrada de Elías, a narrow gorge where the path is carved into the rock above the turquoise water—wooden walkways span the torrent. Higher up, the path passes through forests of centuries-old pines before reaching two mountain lakes, Laguna de Aguas Negras and Laguna de Valdeazores. Allow 6 to 7 hours return. Bring water, food, and good shoes—there's no refreshment along the trail.

activities.text: If you prefer a less physical option, operators offer 4x4 excursions through the park, with stops at viewpoints and wildlife observation points. The Tranco route follows the large dam and crosses pine forests with spectacular views. You can also take a boat trip on the Embalse del Tranco—the artificial lake at the heart of the park. It's from the lake that you have the best chances of seeing deer coming to drink at dusk.

practical_info.tip: 4x4 excursions generally leave in the morning—book the day before with operators in Cazorla

map_keywords: Baños Árabes Segura de la Sierra

visits.text: Final stop: Segura de la Sierra, ranked among Spain's Most Beautiful Villages, perched at 1,230 metres altitude in the park's northeast corner. The village of Muslim origin (conquered in 781 under the name Saqura) has preserved its steep alleyways, vaulted passages, and end-of-the-world atmosphere. The castle, built by the Almohads then transformed by the Order of Santiago in the 13th century, dominates the village from its rocky outcrop. Its 18-metre keep offers a 360° panorama over the sierras, pine forests, and olive groves. The Arab baths, discovered beneath the village, are among the best preserved in the province.

Segura is also the birthplace of the poet Jorge Manrique (15th century), author of Coplas por la muerte de su padre—one of Spanish literature's most famous poems. His father, Don Rodrigo Manrique, was commander of Segura for the Order of Santiago. The poet's birthplace, in the upper part of the village, is open to visitors. Wander the alleyways looking for details: carved coats of arms, Charles III's Imperial Fountain, the Catena Gate, and remnants of the old ramparts.

If you have time before leaving, detour to Hornos de Segura, another spectacular hilltop village 30 minutes' drive away. Its ruined castle dominates the Tranco lake—the view from the village terraces over the lake and mountains is one of the park's most photographed. The Cosmolarium, installed in the castle tower, is a small astronomical observatory and planetarium that takes advantage of the Sierra de Segura's excellent night sky quality.

activities.text: Segura de la Sierra produces a Sierra de Segura PDO olive oil, considered one of Spain's finest. The mountain terroir (700-1,200 m altitude) and the Picual variety produce an intense oil with notes of fresh grass and green tomato. Village cooperatives offer tastings. Leave with a bottle—it's the best souvenir you can bring back from the province of Jaén.

practical_info.tip: From Segura, you can continue to Albacete (2h), Murcia (2h30), or return towards Jaén/Granada (2h)

seo.h1_title: Madrid to Cazorla: La Mancha, the Sierras of Jaén, and the Forgotten Renaissance
seo.intro_paragraph: This 9-day itinerary crosses Spain's great tourist void: the zone between Madrid and Andalusia that nobody visits. Starting from the Prado and Reina Sofía museums, you'll cross Don Quixote's La Mancha to the only intact Golden Age theatre at Almagro, cross the Sierra Morena via Despeñaperros, discover the twin Renaissance towns of Úbeda and Baeza (UNESCO), and finish in Spain's largest natural park, among hilltop villages and spectacular gorges.
seo.seo_hook: From the Prado to Cazorla Park in 9 days: Madrid, Almagro, the Caliphate castle of Burgalimar, UNESCO sites Úbeda and Baeza, Río Borosa gorges, and the perched villages of the Sierra de Segura.
featured_list: Madrid: the Prado (8,000 paintings, Velázquez's Las Meninas) and the Reina Sofía (Picasso's Guernica, 7.76 metres wide)
Almagro: Corral de Comedias from 1628, the only intact Golden Age theatre still active in the world
Úbeda and Baeza: two UNESCO Renaissance towns, Vandelvira masterpieces among the olive groves
Baños de la Encina: Burgalimar Caliphate castle (968), one of Europe's best-preserved Muslim fortresses
Cazorla Park: 210,000 hectares, source of the Guadalquivir, Río Borosa gorges, and hilltop villages

places_taglines.es::madrid: Where the world's finest art collections meet royal palaces and tapas culture
places_taglines.es::almagro: A Golden Age time capsule where Lope de Vega's plays still echo through wooden galleries
places_taglines.es::banos-de-la-encina: Where a thousand-year-old fortress watches over the planet's greatest sea of olives
places_taglines.es::baeza: Renaissance stone and Machado's verses among endless fields of liquid gold
places_taglines.es::ubeda: Where Spanish nobility built a slice of Tuscany in the heart of Andalusia
places_taglines.es::cazorla: The white gateway to Spain's wildest mountains and clearest rivers
places_taglines.es::segura-de-la-sierra: A medieval eagle's nest suspended between earth and sky at 1,230 metres

highlights: Madrid: the Prado Museum (over 8,000 paintings including Velázquez's Las Meninas) and the Royal Palace (3,418 rooms, the largest palace in Western Europe)
Almagro: the Corral de Comedias from 1628, the only intact and still-active Golden Age theatre in the world
Úbeda and Baeza: two Renaissance towns inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage List since 2003, designed by architect Andrés de Vandelvira in the 16th century
Cazorla, Segura y las Villas Natural Park: Spain's largest protected area (210,000 hectares), source of the Guadalquivir

itineraries.title: Alcarria and Guadalajara: Between Madrid and Aragon
itineraries.subtitle: A 6-day itinerary through the great tourist void between Madrid and Zaragoza, from Cervantes' university town to the ramparts of Daroca, via the lavender fields of Alcarria, the fortress-cathedral of Sigüenza, and Medinaceli's unique Roman triple arch.
itineraries.notes: Road trip by car. Brihuega's lavender fields bloom from late June to mid-July—outside this period, the village remains worthwhile for its medieval heritage but you won't see the lavender spectacle. Sigüenza is accessible via a tourist 'Medieval Train' from Madrid on selected Saturdays in spring and autumn.

visits.text: Begin your journey 30 km from Madrid. Alcalá de Henares, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1998, was the world's first purpose-built university town. Cardinal Cisneros founded the university in 1499 with the ambition of creating the City of God (Civitas Dei)—a blueprint later exported to the Americas. Head to the Colegio Mayor de San Ildefonso, the founding building: its Plateresque façade, completed in 1553 by Rodrigo Gil de Hontañón, ranks among Spain's finest. Inside, the Paraninfo (ceremonial hall) hosts the annual Cervantes Prize ceremony every 23 April—the Nobel of Hispanic letters, presented by the King of Spain.

Walk down Calle Mayor, one of Europe's longest arcaded streets, to the Casa Natal de Cervantes. Miguel de Cervantes Saavedra was born here in 1547—his baptismal certificate (9 October 1547) is preserved in the town hall. The house, reconstructed as a 16th-century bourgeois residence, is now a free museum: period furniture, the father's medical office (he was a barber-surgeon), and early editions of Don Quixote. Right next door, don't miss the Corral de Comedias (1601), Spain's oldest documented theatre, restored and still in use.

The Magistral Cathedral of Santos Niños Justo y Pastor is one of only two in the world to bear the title 'Magistral' (the other being Leuven)—meaning all its canons had to be doctors of theology from the university. Built in the 15th–16th centuries in Isabelline Gothic style, it houses Cardinal Cisneros' tomb. Climb the bell tower for views over the town's rooftops and the storks nesting on the spires—Alcalá has one of Spain's largest white stork populations.

activities.text: Spend the late afternoon wandering around Plaza de Cervantes (the old Plaza Mayor), the town's beating heart, lined with cafés and terraces. The central bandstand and colourful façades create a wonderfully lively atmosphere. The university quarter around the Colegio Mayor buzzes with the energy of 25,000 students. Sample the costrada (puff pastry cake filled with cream) and rosquillas (anise doughnuts)—Alcalá's signature sweets.

practical_info.tip: The 'Cervantes Train' runs from Madrid (Chamartín) to Alcalá on selected Saturdays—with costumed actors aboard

visits.text: Drive 45 minutes southeast to Brihuega, nicknamed the 'Garden of Alcarria'. If you visit between late June and mid-July, the lavender fields surrounding the village put on a breathtaking show—endless acres of violet stretching to the horizon, with the Lavender Festival held the second weekend of July. Outside the flowering season, Brihuega still rewards a visit: medieval ramparts with the fortified gates of Cadena and Cozagón, three handsome 13th-century Romanesque churches (Santa María de la Peña, San Miguel, San Felipe), and the former Real Fábrica de Paños, a royal cloth manufactory founded in the 18th century by Charles III.

Continue 30 minutes south to Pastrana, the 'ducal town' of Alcarria, a designated Historic Ensemble since 1966 and member of Spain's Most Beautiful Villages. The Ducal Palace, designed by Alonso de Covarrubias in the 16th century with a façade replicating Toledo's Alcázar, is inseparable from the Princess of Éboli—Ana de Mendoza y de la Cerda. One-eyed (she wore an eyepatch), beautiful, scheming, first Duchess of Pastrana then mistress of Antonio Pérez (Philip II's secretary), she ended up imprisoned by the king in the palace's east tower (the Cámara de la Reja Dorada) from 1581 until her death in 1592. Look up at the barred window from Plaza de la Hora—that's where she watched the world go by.

Don't leave Pastrana without visiting the Tapestry Museum in the collegiate church of the Asunción. The six 15th-century Flemish-Gothic tapestries, woven in Tournai from cartoons attributed to Nuño Gonçalves, depict the conquest of North African cities by King Afonso V of Portugal. They rank among the world's most important tapestries from this period. The collegiate church itself, of Templar origin (12th century), holds paintings by El Greco and Carreño de Miranda. The Princess of Éboli is buried in the crypt.

activities.text: Pastrana is also linked to Saint Teresa of Ávila, who founded two Discalced Carmelite convents here in 1569—one for women (San José) and one for men (San Pablo, now del Carmen), where Saint John of the Cross served as novice master. The Carmen convent houses a small Teresian museum and a natural history museum. Stroll through the lanes of the upper town, between Calle Princesa de Éboli lined with noble mansions and Calle de la Palma with the House of the Inquisition and the House of the Calatrava Knight.

practical_info.tip: Pastrana can easily be covered in half a day—combine it with Brihuega in the morning

visits.text: Head north across the Alcarria plateau. After 45 minutes, stop at Cifuentes (population 2,000). This quiet town boasts a 14th-century castle built by Don Juan Manuel—a lettered prince, nephew of Alfonso X the Wise, and author of El Conde Lucanor. The castle, square-planned with round corner towers, dominates the Cifuentes valley. Below, the arcaded Plaza Mayor and the church of El Salvador (13th century), transitional Romanesque-Gothic, round out the visit. Allow 45 minutes.

Drive 30 minutes onwards to Sigüenza, one of Castile's hidden gems. Perched at 1,000 m altitude, this town of 4,500 souls, designated a Historic and Artistic Monument, is crowned by its 12th-century castle—now one of Spain's finest Paradores. Built on an Arab citadel which itself stood on a Roman site, it hosted the Catholic Monarchs and survived the Napoleonic Wars, Carlist Wars, and Civil War. Even if you're not staying overnight, climb up to see the cobbled courtyard, the 13th-century Romanesque chapel, and the vaulted dining hall.

The Cathedral of Santa María de Sigüenza is a fortress-cathedral—its two massive façade towers give it the air of a castle keep. Begun in 1124 after the reconquest from the Muslims, it blends Romanesque, Gothic, and Renaissance styles. But it's the tomb of the Doncel that justifies your pilgrimage. Martín Vázquez de Arce, a page to Isabella the Catholic who died in battle in 1486 aged 25, is sculpted in alabaster in a unique pose: not lying asleep like other recumbent effigies, but half-reclining, one leg crossed over the other, reading a book. It's one of the most celebrated funerary sculptures of Spanish Gothic, a symbol of wisdom wedded to strength.

activities.text: Take your time descending from the castle to Plaza Mayor through the cobbled lanes of Travesaña Alta, the medieval quarter. Plaza Mayor, laid out in the 15th century, is surrounded by arcades and noble blazoned mansions. Push on to the Casa del Doncel (15th century), the former residence of the Vázquez de Arce family, with its crenellated façade—it now hosts exhibitions. In the evening, the plaza's terraces come gently to life in the golden sunset light—Sigüenza is a town to savour at a slow pace.

practical_info.tip: The Parador is a splendid option for dinner even if you're not staying—book a table in the vaulted hall

activities.text: If you have time the following morning before departing, the Barranco del Río Dulce Natural Park (10 km from Sigüenza) offers a limestone canyon with griffon vultures, golden eagles, and hiking trails between the cliffs. The village of Pelegrina, clinging to the canyon walls, is worth the detour.

practical_info.tip: Optional but superb. Marked trail between Pelegrina and La Cabrera, about 1h30 one-way

visits.text: Head northeast. After 30 minutes, you reach Atienza, a fortified village clinging to a rocky outcrop at 1,169 m altitude. Seven medieval gates still pierce its ramparts (including the Arco de Arrebatacapas and the Puerta de la Virgen). At the summit, the castle ruins—described by Lonely Planet as seeming like 'a natural extension of the rock'—command a complete panorama over the Guadalajara sierras. Atienza preserves several Romanesque churches, including San Gil (12th century) now converted into a sacred art museum. Plaza del Trigo (Wheat Square), lined with half-timbered houses, is the village heart.

Continue 50 minutes east to enter Soria province and reach Medinaceli. This hilltop village at 1,200 m altitude bears the legacy of every civilization that passed through: Celtiberian, Roman, Visigothic, Arab, Castilian. Its star monument is the Roman Arch, dating from the 1st century AD—the only triple-arched monumental gateway preserved in Spain. Set atop the hill facing the Jalón valley, it measures roughly 13 m long and 8 m high. Its exact dating is debated—long thought to be Trajanic, recent research attributes it more likely to the Domitian era.

Beyond the arch, wander through Medinaceli (population 700) which harbours dense heritage: the Collegiate Church of Santa María de la Asunción (16th century), the Palace of the Dukes of Medinaceli on Plaza Mayor (hosting contemporary art exhibitions), the convent of Santa Isabel, and remnants of the Arab walls. Plaza Mayor, with its arcades and discreet evening lighting, has an atmosphere out of time. This is also where Al-Mansur (Almanzor), the fearsome military leader of the Caliphate of Córdoba, supposedly died in 1002, after his defeat at Calatañazor.

activities.text: Savour Medinaceli's tranquillity in the late afternoon. The village is almost deserted—perfect for walking along the ramparts with views over the Jalón valley and the Castilian plateaux. If you're a foodie, the region produces Soria lamb, seasonal mushrooms, and black truffles in winter.

practical_info.tip: Medinaceli is tiny—rural accommodation in the vicinity (Arcos de Jalón, Almazán) may be more practical

visits.text: Twenty-five minutes' drive east from Medinaceli brings you to the Monastery of Santa María de Huerta, one of Spain's most beautiful Cistercian monasteries. Founded in 1162, expropriated in 1835 during the Mendizábal disentailment, then returned to the order in 1930, it's still home to some twenty monks. Push through the door and you enter eight centuries of silence. The Gothic cloister (13th century), with its pointed arcades and austere garden, prepares you for the shock of the refectory—an immense hall covered with sexpartite rib vaults, pierced by tall windows that flood the space with light. It's one of Europe's most beautiful monastic refectories.

Continue 1h15 west to enter Aragon and reach Calatayud, second city of Zaragoza province. The ancient Bilbilis (birthplace of the poet Martial) is dominated by the ruins of the Castillo de Ayub, the Arab citadel that gave the town its name (Qal'at Ayyub—Ayyub's castle). But it's Aragonese Mudéjar that makes Calatayud rich: the Collegiate Church of Santa María la Mayor boasts a unique open-air cloister and a Mudéjar brick portal decorated with ceramics. Calatayud's Mudéjar art forms part of the UNESCO inscription of Mudéjar Architecture of Aragon (2001).

Stroll through Calatayud's historic centre, between Plaza de España (with its fountain and arcades) and the lanes climbing towards the castle ruins. The church of San Pedro de los Francos (14th century), with its leaning Mudéjar tower (yes, it leans—about 2 degrees), is the building where the Aragonese Cortes proclaimed Ferdinand the Catholic heir to the throne in 1461. The medieval Jewish cemetery, discovered in 1980, testifies to the significant Sephardic community that lived here before the 1492 expulsion.

activities.text: If you're a wine lover, Calatayud sits at the heart of the DO Calatayud, an appellation producing Garnacha from old vines growing on slate soils at 700–1,000 m altitude. Several cellars offer tastings. Pair your glass with Aragonese tapas: migas, roast ternasco lamb, or frutas de Aragón (candied fruits coated in chocolate).

practical_info.tip: Book ahead with the wineries. DO Calatayud is renowned for its old-vine Garnacha

map_keywords: Fuente de los Veinte Caños Daroca

visits.text: Final stop: Daroca, 1h drive southeast from Calatayud through the Jiloca valley. This town of 2,000 inhabitants possesses one of Spain's longest fortified enclosures: 4 km of ramparts bristling with 114 towers, built from the 9th century by the Arabs and enlarged after the Christian conquest by Alfonso I the Battler in 1120. Enter through the Puerta Baja (14th century), an impressive gate-tunnel that also served as a river drain—note the Fuente de los Veinte Caños (Fountain of Twenty Spouts) just outside. Climb up Calle Mayor, the town's backbone, between Mudéjar brick churches (San Miguel, San Juan, Santo Domingo) and noble palaces.

The Basilica of Santa María de los Sagrados Corporales, at the town's heart, is one of Aragon's most remarkable. It houses the relics of a Eucharistic miracle that occurred in 1239 during the Battle of Chío, when six consecrated hosts supposedly survived intact wrapped in the corporals despite the heat of battle. The main altarpiece, the Chapel of the Corporals, and the adjacent museum merit careful attention. Exit through the Puerta Alta (15th century), adorned with Charles V's coat of arms.

activities.text: Climb up to the Castillo Mayor via the path that follows the ramparts. Allow 1h30 round-trip. The wall-walk lets you link several towers and offers plunging views over the town squeezed between two ridges and the Jiloca valley. From the castle, the panorama embraces cereal fields, red clay hills and, on clear days, the sierras of the Iberian System. Bring good shoes and water—no shade on the ramparts.

practical_info.tip: Avoid the hottest hours in summer. The Museo de la Pastelería Manuel Segura, in town, offers a welcome sweet break

activities.text: From Daroca, you can continue towards Teruel (1h30 south) to join the 'Aragonese Pyrenees and Lower Aragon' itinerary (UNESCO Mudéjar towers), or head back up to Zaragoza (1h15 north). Daroca is also a starting point for the village of Anento (15 km), member of Spain's Most Beautiful Villages, with its waterfall and hanging gardens.

practical_info.tip: Anento is tiny and can be visited in 1h—perfect as a detour before hitting the road again

seo.h1_title: Alcarria and Guadalajara: Between Madrid and Aragon
seo.intro_paragraph: Between Madrid and Zaragoza stretches one of Spain's great tourist voids—Guadalajara province and the borderlands of Soria. This 6-day itinerary crosses this little-known territory from Cervantes' university town (Alcalá de Henares, UNESCO) to the ramparts of Daroca, taking in Pastrana's Flemish tapestries, Brihuega's lavender fields, Sigüenza's fortress-cathedral with the celebrated tomb of the Doncel, Medinaceli's triple-arched Roman gateway, and the Cistercian monastery of Santa María de Huerta.
seo.seo_hook: Alcalá de Henares to Daroca in 6 days: Cervantes, the Princess of Éboli, Alcarria lavender, Sigüenza's Doncel, Medinaceli's Roman arch, and 4 km of medieval ramparts.
featured_list: Alcalá de Henares: world's first purpose-built university town (1499), UNESCO, birthplace of Cervantes
Pastrana: ducal palace of the Princess of Éboli and 15th-century Flemish tapestries among the world's most important
Sigüenza: fortress-cathedral and tomb of the Doncel, one of Spanish Gothic's finest funerary sculptures
Medinaceli: Spain's only preserved triple-arched Roman gateway, 1st century AD
Daroca: 4 km of medieval ramparts bristling with 114 towers

places_taglines.es::alcala-de-henares: Where Cervantes drew his first breath and scholars built the world's first purpose-planned university town.
places_taglines.es::pastrana: The ducal stronghold where the one-eyed Princess of Éboli plotted, prayed, and died behind gilded bars.
places_taglines.es::siguenza: A fortress-cathedral cradling Spain's most moving sculpture: a young knight frozen reading for eternity.
places_taglines.es::medinaceli: Where the only triple-arched Roman gateway in Spain still crowns a windswept Castilian ridge.
places_taglines.es::calatayud: Mudéjar towers lean above vineyards where old Garnacha vines cling to slate at a thousand metres.
places_taglines.es::daroca: Four kilometres of ramparts and 114 towers march across the hills in silent medieval defiance.

seo_keywords: Santa María de Huerta Cistercian monastery

highlights: Alcalá de Henares: world's first purpose-built university town (1499), UNESCO, birthplace of Cervantes
Sigüenza: fortress-cathedral and tomb of the Doncel, one of Spanish Gothic's finest funerary sculptures
Medinaceli: Spain's only preserved triple-arched Roman gateway, 1st century AD
Daroca: 4 km of medieval ramparts bristling with 114 towers, one of Spain's longest fortified enclosures

itineraries.title: La Mancha Road Trip: Don Quixote's Windmills and Cuenca
itineraries.subtitle: From Cuenca to Ruidera, between vertiginous gorges, Cervantine windmills and turquoise lagoons in the heart of the Meseta
itineraries.notes: Dramatic cliffs and hanging towns, iconic windmills and emerald lagoons in the heart of Don Quixote country.
specialties: Manchego cheese (sheep's milk, aged in caves)
Saffron from La Mancha (world's finest crocus)

map_keywords: Museo de Arte Abstracto Español Cuenca

visits.text: The Casas Colgadas cling to the cliffside above the Huécar gorge, their wooden balconies jutting out into thin air as if floating in space. Inside, the Museum of Spanish Abstract Art showcases works by Zóbel, Tàpies and Chillida in rooms that once hung over the void.

The Cathedral of Santa María y San Julián dominates Plaza Mayor with its unlikely fusion of Norman Gothic, Romanesque elements and neo-Gothic additions. Begun in the late 12th century, its main façade opens onto the arcaded square at the heart of the old town.
Cross the Puente de San Pablo, a metal footbridge suspended 60 meters above the Huécar gorge. It offers the best view of the Hanging Houses, whether by day or at night when they're dramatically lit.

Climb back up through the steep lanes of the UNESCO-listed Casco Antiguo. Colorful façades stack one above another, forming 'suspended favelas' over the abyss—the medieval fabric remains intact here, without the crowds of Toledo.
The Fundación Antonio Pérez occupies a former 17th-century convent. Spanish and international contemporary art displayed in a setting of bare stone and exposed beams.

30 km from Cuenca, the Ciudad Encantada unfolds its karst formations sculpted by 250 million years of erosion. The dolomite rocks take the shapes of animals, faces and natural arches along a marked trail of about 3 km.

activities.text: Follow the trail that runs along the Huécar gorge below the old town. The path winds between limestone cliffs and vegetation, offering striking perspectives on the hanging houses seen from below.
Stop at the Ventano del Diablo, a natural balcony carved into the rock that overlooks the Júcar gorge. The plunging view of the green waters and limestone cliffs is spectacular.
Try the morteruelo, a warm pâté made with pork liver and spices—Cuenca's signature dish. Pair it with aged Manchego cheese and a wine from the Serranía.

visits.text: Twelve white windmills line the ridge of Cerro Calderico. Built in the late 16th century by the Order of Saint John of Jerusalem, each bears the name of a character from Don Quixote: Sancho, Rocinante, Clavileño…

Push open the door of the Sancho mill, the best preserved of the dozen. Its original mechanism still functions and grinds grain during the Fiesta de la Rosa del Azafrán on the last weekend of October.

The castle of La Muela dominates the hill alongside the windmills. This 10th-century fortress of Arab origin became the seat of the Knights of the Order of Saint John. Its restored halls and ramparts offer a 360° panorama over the plains of the Meseta.

Descend into the village of Consuegra. Plaza de España, a former Roman forum, hosts a Renaissance town hall and the 17th-century Los Corredores building, typical of Manchegan architecture with its wooden galleries.
The church of San Juan Bautista and the cobbled lanes of the historic center reveal several Mudéjar hermitages and whitewashed houses with ochre tile roofs.

activities.text: Head to Cerro Calderico in late afternoon. The golden light illuminates the white windmills against a backdrop of endless plains—the perfect moment to photograph this icon of La Mancha.
The Consuegra region is one of the main terroirs of Spanish saffron. Local cooperatives offer tastings of olive oil and Manchego saffron.
Walk up from the parking lot at the base of the hill (20 min). The gradual ascent reveals the windmills and castle one by one, adding suspense to the panorama.

visits.text: Almagro's Plaza Mayor is bordered by arcades with colonnades and windows framed in green—legacy of the Fugger family, German bankers to Charles V. One of the finest medieval ensembles in Castile.
The Corral de Comedias, built in 1628, is the only intact Golden Age Spanish theater still standing. Its 300 m² patio, two-story wooden galleries and original stage host performances year-round.
Take a seat in the 'cazuela', the balcony once reserved for women, to understand the 17th-century segregation of the sexes. Beneath the stage, the 'bodega' served as the actors' dressing room.

The National Theater Museum occupies the Master's Palaces of the Order of Calatrava, built in the 13th century. Permanent collections trace the history of Spanish theater from Baroque corrales to contemporary stages.

The Almacén de los Fúcares, a 16th-century Renaissance warehouse that belonged to the Fugger bankers, hosts summer performances in its interior courtyard. Noble palaces dot the district: Marquis of Torremejía, Counts of Valdeparaíso.

activities.text: On weekends, attend a theatrical tour of the Corral de Comedias where costumed actors reenact scenes from the Golden Age in the original setting. In July, the International Classical Theater Festival takes over all of Almagro.
Tapas bars around Plaza Mayor serve Manchegan specialties: pisto, migas, aged Manchego cheese. Almagro is also renowned for its pickled eggplants (berenjenas de Almagro).
Valdepeñas is 25 km away. Local cellars offer tours and tastings of DO Valdepeñas wines—reds aged in oak barrels from Tempranillo grapes.

practical_info.tip: Booking preferable

activities.text: Almagro is a major center for bobbin lace and blonda. Shops in the historic center display local creations.

map_keywords: Molinos de Viento Campo de Criptana
Sierra de los Molinos Campo de Criptana
Cerro de la Paz Campo de Criptana
Ermita de la Virgen de Criptana

visits.text: Ten white windmills stand atop the Sierra de los Molinos, remnants of the 32 that dominated the town in the 16th century. Three—Infante, Sardinero and Burleta—retain their period machinery intact and are designated national monuments.
The Poyatos mill serves as both tourist office and interpretation center. Inside, models and maps explain the workings of Manchegan windmills and how the blades were oriented to face the wind.

From the Cerro de la Paz, the view sweeps across the white rooftops of the village and the immensity of the Castilian plain. This is where Cervantes set Don Quixote's battle against the 'thirty outrageous giants.'

Descend toward the Pósito Real, a 16th-century grain warehouse expanded under Charles III. Its façade bears two coats of arms of the Order of Santiago. It regulated the price of grain during periods of scarcity.
Cave houses carved into the rock line the windmill route. The Casa Cueva de Mambrino houses a museum that recreates troglodyte life with agricultural tools and 16th-century utensils.

The hermitage of the Virgen de Criptana, in Renaissance style, has dominated the hill since the 16th century. Below, the hermitage of Santa Ana and that of the Vera Cruz complete the religious heritage.

activities.text: Arrive at dawn on the Sierra de los Molinos. The absence of vegetation, arid soil and low morning light create an austere, photogenic landscape—the windmills stand out one by one against the horizon.
The Bodega Cooperativa Vinícola del Carmen, founded in 1897, is considered the oldest wine cooperative in Spain. Taste DO La Mancha wines made from Tempranillo and Airén grapes.

practical_info.tip: Booking recommended

activities.text: Sample gachas manchegas, a flour porridge accompanied by chorizo and fried lard, and Manchegan ratatouille (pisto), typical regional dishes.

visits.text: The Lagunas de Ruidera natural park strings together 15 lagoons over 20 km along the upper course of the Guadiana, separated by natural travertine barriers. The total elevation drop of 128 meters creates a succession of waterfalls between each basin.
Start at the Cascada del Hundimiento, the park's highest waterfall, just steps from the village of Ruidera. It's the starting point of the left-bank trail, the wildest and best-preserved route.
The Laguna del Rey is the park's deepest lagoon. From the Mirador de la Laguna del Rey, the view sweeps across its turquoise waters bordered by reeds. A developed beach allows swimming in summer.

The Laguna Colgada ('hanging' lagoon) extends into a tongue of land that advances into the water. At its tip, small waterfalls feed the Laguna de Batana below—a bucolic landscape typical of tufa formations.
The Laguna Lengua offers the park's most spectacular colors: deep blue and emerald green. The limestone rock barriers and waterfalls carving their way through create an almost surreal setting.

The Cueva de Montesinos, a cave mentioned by Cervantes in Don Quixote, opens onto the plateau of Campo de Montiel. This is where the errant knight claimed to have spent three days in an enchanted palace.

activities.text: Paddle a kayak on the Laguna del Rey or Laguna Colgada. The calm, crystal-clear waters allow you to glide along the travertine cliffs and access beaches and caves unreachable on foot.

practical_info.tip: Rentals available on-site in Ruidera

activities.text: Hike the left-bank trail between the Cascada del Hundimiento and Laguna Redondilla. The path crosses shaded zones and offers plunging views over the successive lagoons.
At dawn, the lagoons attract grey herons, coots, ducks and raptors. The marsh reeds and quiet shores of the park make excellent terrain for birdwatching.

practical_info.tip: Binoculars recommended

activities.text: Set up a picnic in the designated areas by the lagoons. The turquoise waters and birdsong compose an ideal setting for a nature lunch break.

seo.h1_title: La Mancha Road Trip: Don Quixote's Windmills and Cuenca
seo.intro_paragraph: The hanging houses of Cuenca, the windmills of Consuegra and Campo de Criptana, the theater of Almagro, the lakes of Ruidera. La Mancha, in the footsteps of Don Quixote.
seo.seo_hook: Windmills turn on golden, parched plateaus, gorge waters tumble below, and Don Quixote's dreams haunt the landscape. La Mancha is timeless.
featured_list: Cuenca: houses suspended over gorges
Consuegra: windmills crowning iconic hilltop
Almagro: theater on medieval plaza
Lagunas: chain of interconnected lakes

places_taglines.ES::cuenca: Where medieval houses cling to cliffsides between two dramatic gorges
places_taglines.ES::consuegra: A ridge of twelve white windmills commanding the golden plains of the Meseta
places_taglines.ES::almagro: Spain's theatrical soul preserved in a 17th-century corral
places_taglines.ES::campo-de-criptana: Where Don Quixote's giants still turn their sails against the sky
places_taglines.ES::lagunas-de-ruidera: Fifteen turquoise lagoons cascading through travertine terraces

highlights: Casas Colgadas of Cuenca and Huécar gorges (UNESCO)
Twelve windmills of Consuegra on Cerro Calderico
Corral de Comedias of Almagro, unique intact 17th-century theater
Fifteen turquoise lagoons of Ruidera linked by waterfalls

itineraries.title: The Don Quixote Route: A Road Trip Through Castilla-La Mancha's Windmills & Plains

map_keywords: Mosque of Cristo de la Luz

visits.text: Discover the magnificent Toledo Cathedral, one of the most impressive in Spain with its blend of Gothic, Mudéjar and Renaissance styles. Don't miss the Chapel of the Kings, the famous gold Monstrance, the magnificent paintings in the Sacristy, and the Choir. An essential visit in the city of the three cultures.

Explore the imposing Alcázar of Toledo, a fortress dominating the city from above. Climb to one of its towers to enjoy a spectacular panoramic view of Toledo from the sky. It currently houses the Castilla-La Mancha Library and the Army Museum.

Visit the Santa Cruz Museum, a complex formed by the former Hospital of Santa Cruz and the Convent of Santa Fe. The Hospital building features an impressive courtyard, a marble staircase by Covarrubias, and enormous interior arches well worth contemplating.

Admire El Greco's masterpiece, 'The Burial of the Count of Orgaz', in the Church of Santo Tomé. This 16th-century painting is one of the jewels of Spanish art and masterfully represents the mysticism of the era with its composition divided between the earthly and celestial worlds.

activities.text: Wander through Toledo's Jewish Quarter, one of the most charming neighborhoods in the city. Visit the Synagogue of El Tránsito, home to the Sephardic Museum, and the Santa María la Blanca Synagogue with its filigree work and capitals that amaze everyone. Discover the legacy of Jewish culture in Spain.

Stroll through the convent district, where calm and silence reign. In some convents you can buy sweets handcrafted by the nuns. Discover the elevated passageways that the congregations used to move from one building to another unseen.

Visit the Mosque of Cristo de la Luz, the Puerta del Sol, and the Puerta de Bisagra. At sunset, take a taxi to the Parador de Toledo to enjoy superb panoramic views of the city during sunset—the best reward after a day of sightseeing.

visits.text: Behold the famous windmills of Consuegra, the giants of the ingenious gentleman of La Mancha perched on Calderico Hill. Of the thirteen original windmills, twelve have been restored, each with its own name. Visit the interior of the Bolero windmill to understand its workings and layout as a dwelling.

Explore Consuegra Castle, whose origins date back to the era of the Caliphate of Córdoba. It was the headquarters of the Order of Saint John and after its destruction by Napoleonic troops was partially restored. Visit its gallery, cisterns, towers, archive hall, and chapter house.

Discover the Plaza de España with its 1670 Town Hall, where the arch and clock tower stand out. In the same square you'll find the Municipal Museum with archaeological remains from the Neolithic period to the late 19th century.

activities.text: Visit the Church of Cristo de la Veracruz, whose construction began in 1550, with a longitudinal structure and Latin cross plan in Castilian-Mudéjar style. Also stroll by the Prioral Palace and the Casa de la Tercia, the former house of the Order of Saint John.

visits.text: Visit the Corral de Comedias of Almagro, the only complete and intact Golden Age theater in the world. This rectangular building with two floors of galleries was where masters like Lope de Vega, Mateo Alemán, and Tirso de Molina staged their works. Since 1977, it has been the home of the International Festival of Classical Theater.

Stroll through Almagro's Plaza Mayor with its arcades, glazed galleries, and the equestrian statue of Don Diego de Almagro, conqueror of Peru and Captain General of the Kingdom of Chile. The uniformity of the three-story architectural ensemble is impressive.

Explore the Maestral Palace, commissioned by the Order of Calatrava in the 13th century atop a small Arab castle. It was the Mother House of this powerful Order and now houses the National Theater Museum. Its Mudéjar courtyard with horseshoe arches is extraordinary.

Discover the 16th-century Fugger Warehouse, built as a warehouse and office by Emperor Charles V's bankers. The two-story courtyard with semicircular arches and the conventual staircase decorated with egg-and-dart, arrows, and sea wave motifs are magnificent.

activities.text: Visit the Church of San Agustín, an 18th-century Baroque building with impressive frescoes depicting the life of Saint Augustine. The dome over the crossing is a true masterpiece of lacework, leaving practically no space empty of painting.

Explore the Palace of the Counts of Valdeparaíso, a 17th-century Baroque building, and the Palace of the Marquises of Torremegía from the 15th-17th centuries. Don't miss the 16th-century Hermitage of San Blas, rebuilt by the Fuggers, and the Monastery of the Concepción Bernarda.

map_keywords: Molinos de Viento Campo de Criptana
Sierra de los Molinos Campo de Criptana
Cerro de la Paz Campo de Criptana
Ermita de la Virgen de Criptana

visits.text: Admire the windmills of Campo de Criptana, the very ones that inspired Cervantes in his famous novel. Of the 34 original windmills, 10 remain, and three of them preserve their original 16th-century machinery and workings. An authentic Quixotic treasure on the Sierra de los Molinos.

Visit the cave-house of the shepherdess Marcela, which preserves its original structure with intact walls. Discover the different rooms: bedrooms, storage, pantry, and the central room with fireplace. A unique way of life carved into the rock of La Mancha.

Explore the Sara Montiel Museum in the Culebro Windmill, dedicated to the life of this actress, singer, and film producer born in Campo de Criptana. See photographs, dresses, and even her grand piano while learning interesting facts about her artistic career.

activities.text: Wander through the Albaicín neighborhood of Campo de Criptana with its narrow, cobbled, and steep streets, its houses with Arab tiles and walls painted white and indigo blue. The panoramic views of the Sierra de los Molinos from this neighborhood are superb.

Visit the 16th-century Royal Granary, the former headquarters of the agricultural bank where loans were made in kind to farmers. It now houses the Archaeological Museum of Campo de Criptana. Don't miss the Convent Church and the Hermitage of the Veracruz from 1573.

map_keywords: Museo de Arte Abstracto Español Cuenca

visits.text: Contemplate the iconic Hanging Houses of Cuenca, symbol of the city with their large cantilevered balconies over the Huécar River gorge. Inside you'll find the Museum of Spanish Abstract Art. Cross the San Pablo Bridge for the best panoramic view, both by day and by night.

Visit the Cathedral of Santa María and San Julián of Cuenca, predominantly Gothic but with Romanesque and Neo-Gothic elements. Its main façade faces the Plaza Mayor and its interior houses artistic treasures of great value. One of the most unique cathedrals in Spain.

Discover the skyscrapers of Cuenca, a group of 30 buildings that have 4-5 floors on one side and, facing the Huécar Gorge, rise to 8-10 floors on the other. A singular and striking architectural ensemble unique to Spain.

Walk along Alfonso VIII Street and descend through Las Angustias until you find the Hermitage of Our Lady of the Sorrows. Wander through the historic center declared a World Heritage Site by UNESCO and discover the castle and Calle de los Tintes along the Huécar River.

activities.text: Visit the Enchanted City, a spectacular site with rock formations sculpted over thousands of years by wind, water, and ice. A 20-meter monolith, the Roman Bridge, and the face of a man are some of the 13 figures along the trail. Nearby is the Devil's Window viewpoint with views of the Júcar River canyon.

Discover the source of the Cuervo River via a trail through willows and hazel trees leading to two breathtaking waterfalls, especially impressive during rainy months. Thirty minutes away, the source of the Júcar River in Tragacete offers a 20-meter waterfall and an old mill.

seo.h1_title: The Don Quixote Route: A Road Trip Through Castilla-La Mancha's Windmills & Plains
seo.intro_paragraph: Step into the pages of literature on this unforgettable 5-day adventure through Castilla-La Mancha, the land that inspired Cervantes' immortal knight. From the imperial grandeur of Toledo to the iconic windmills of La Mancha and the gravity-defying Hanging Houses of Cuenca, this itinerary weaves together UNESCO World Heritage Sites, medieval castles, and timeless landscapes that have fired imaginations for centuries.
seo.seo_hook: Where windmills become giants and every hilltop tells a tale—discover the real La Mancha that sparked the world's first modern novel.
featured_list: Toledo: The City of Three Cultures with El Greco's masterpieces
Iconic windmills of Consuegra and Campo de Criptana
Almagro's Golden Age theater, the only one intact in the world
Cuenca's dramatic Hanging Houses perched over the gorge
The Enchanted City's surreal rock formations
UNESCO World Heritage historic centers

places_taglines.ES::toledo: Imperial city where three cultures forged Spain's soul
places_taglines.ES::consuegra: Where Don Quixote's giants stand guard over La Mancha
places_taglines.ES::almagro: Golden Age theater capital frozen in time
places_taglines.ES::campo-de-criptana: The windmills that inspired Cervantes' immortal tale

itineraries.title: Castilian Duero: From Valladolid to Cuenca via Ribera and Alcarria
itineraries.subtitle: A 10-day road trip through Spain's forgotten heartland, exploring baroque polychrome sculpture, Reconquest castles, Ribera del Duero vineyards, ducal villages, and Alcarria's lavender fields.
itineraries.notes: This itinerary crosses three autonomous communities (Castile and León, Castile-La Mancha, Castile-La Mancha/Guadalajara). A vehicle is essential—public transport between small towns is scarce. Brihuega's lavender fields bloom in July only. Book wine tastings and cellar visits in advance.

visits.text: Begin your exploration of Valladolid at the Museo Nacional de Escultura, one of Europe's most distinctive museums. Housed in the 15th-century College of Saint Gregory—an Isabelline-style masterpiece whose façade is a triumph of carved stonework—it holds over 6,000 pieces of polychrome wooden sculpture from the 15th to 18th centuries. You'll encounter works by the three great Castilian masters: Alonso Berruguete, pioneer of Spanish Mannerism who studied under Michelangelo in Florence; Juan de Juni, famed for his dramatic compositions; and Gregorio Fernández, whose Passion Christs are still carried in procession through Valladolid's streets every Holy Week. Don't miss Berruguete's Great Altarpiece of San Benito (16th century) or the 17th-century processional pasos. The museum spans three buildings—allow at least 2 hours, more if you're passionate about sacred art.

Stepping out of the museum, look up at the façade of San Pablo church, directly opposite. This Isabelline stone lacework, completed in the late 15th century, rivals that of the College of Saint Gregory. The interior is more austere but preserves an impressive Baroque altarpiece. This is where Philip II (1527) and Philip IV (1605) were baptized—Valladolid served as Spain's capital twice, under Charles V and briefly under Philip III between 1601 and 1606.

Walk down to the cathedral, designed by Juan de Herrera—the architect of El Escorial—in the late 16th century. The project was so ambitious it was never completed: only part of the nave and one tower were built. The interior, despite its unfinished appearance, houses a monumental altarpiece by Juan de Juni. Climb to the Diocesan Museum in the cloister for a fine collection of painting and sculpture.

Head next to the Plaza Mayor, one of Spain's first arcaded squares, laid out in the 16th century. This is where the Catholic Monarchs Ferdinand and Isabella celebrated their wedding in 1469. Stroll beneath the porticoes, grab a coffee on a terrace, and observe the statue of Christopher Columbus at the center. The explorer died in Valladolid on May 20, 1506, convinced to the end that he had reached Asia. His remains have since traveled between Seville, Santo Domingo, Havana, and back to Seville—almost as much as the man himself during his lifetime.

Finish with a stroll through Campo Grande, Valladolid's great urban park—115,000 m² of greenery in the heart of the city. Peacocks roam freely, monumental fountains punctuate tree-lined paths shaded by century-old specimens. It's the perfect place to catch your breath before dinner.

activities.text: Valladolid is a city of tapas and wine. Head to the streets around Plaza Mayor and Calle Correos for a pinchos crawl—Castilian-style tapas. The local specialty is lechazo asado, milk-fed lamb roasted in wood-fired ovens, served in traditional asadores. Pair it with a glass of Ribera del Duero or Rueda, the province's two great appellations.

practical_info.tip: The area around Calle Correos and Plaza del Val has the best pinchos spots

map_keywords: Real Monasterio de Santa Clara Tordesillas

visits.text: A thirty-minute drive from Valladolid brings you to Tordesillas, perched on the banks of the Duero. This small town witnessed one of world history's most decisive moments: on June 7, 1494, Spain and Portugal signed the treaty that divided the world between the two crowns, drawing a line 370 leagues west of the Cape Verde Islands. Everything west went to Spain, everything east to Portugal—this treaty explains why Brazil speaks Portuguese today. The Museo del Tratado, housed in the Casas del Tratado, recounts this episode through period documents and maps. The original treaty is listed in UNESCO's Memory of the World Register.

The Real Monasterio de Santa Clara is Tordesillas's star monument. This Mudéjar palace, built in the 14th century by Alfonso XI and transformed into a convent by his son Peter the Cruel, showcases Mudéjar art—Christian art created by Muslim artisans—in all its splendor: carved stucco, azulejos, 15th-century Arab baths, and an arcaded patio reminiscent of the Alhambra. But another episode haunts these walls: Joanna I of Castile, called Joanna the Mad, was imprisoned here from 1509 to 1555—46 years of captivity ordered by her own father, Ferdinand of Aragon, then maintained by her son Charles V. She left the palace only to reach San Antolín church via an underground passage. The guided tour lasts about an hour.

Climb to the church of San Antolín, now converted into a museum of sacred art. From its tower—the 57 steps that Joanna the Mad climbed to show herself to her people, according to legend—the view over the Duero and the Castilian plain is superb. On your way down, wander the lanes around the Plaza Mayor: you'll notice modern artistic graffiti depicting historical scenes, including a face-off between Joanna and her son Charles V.

Cross the medieval bridge over the Duero—ten stone arches spanning the river since the 13th century. It's one of Castile's finest bridges. From the opposite bank, Tordesillas's silhouette with its bell towers and ramparts offers a highly photogenic view, especially late in the day.

activities.text: Tordesillas is renowned for its Castilian gastronomy: lechazo (milk-fed lamb), judiones (large white beans), and rosquillas (traditional doughnuts). Settle into one of the arcaded restaurants on Plaza Mayor for an unhurried lunch, as befits the rhythm of the Meseta.

practical_info.tip: On Sundays, the market on the square is lively and restaurants fill up—arrive early

map_keywords: Colegiata Santa María la Mayor Toro
Iglesia San Lorenzo el Real Toro

visits.text: Toro rises on a cliff above the Duero, 30 minutes west of Tordesillas. The Collegiate Church of Santa María la Mayor, begun in the late 12th century, is the major monument. Its model is Zamora Cathedral: a tower-lantern crowned by a ribbed dome, typical of the Duero valley Romanesque. But it's the Portada de la Majestad, the west portico completed around 1240, that alone justifies the journey: over 125 carved figurines and—exceptionally rare—they still preserve their original polychrome from eight centuries ago. It's the only intact polychrome Romanesque portico in Spain.

Tour Toro's Romanesque-Mudéjar churches: San Lorenzo el Real (12th century), the oldest, with its brick walls; El Salvador, San Pedro del Olmo, and the hermitage of Santa María de la Vega. Toro had 40 churches in the Middle Ages—about twenty remain, some in poetic ruin. The Alcázar, partially restored, offers a plunging view over the Duero and the vineyards below.

Descend to the medieval bridge spanning the Duero below the town. From the bridge, look up: Toro's silhouette perched on its cliff, with the collegiate's dome rising from the rooftops, is one of the most spectacular sights in Duero Castile.

activities.text: Toro is the heart of DO Toro, a wine appellation whose wines already traveled in Christopher Columbus's holds. The local grape, Tinta de Toro—a cousin of Tempranillo—grows on sandy soils that protected it from phylloxera in the 19th century. Some vines are pre-phylloxera, among Europe's oldest. Operators offer cellar visits with tastings. The town's subsoil hides over 300 medieval underground cellars—some can be visited.

practical_info.tip: Book cellar visits in advance—many only receive guests by appointment

map_keywords: Iglesia San Claudio de Olivares Zamora

visits.text: Zamora is Spain's Romanesque capital: some thirty Romanesque buildings cluster within a compact area, designated a historic ensemble. Start with the cathedral, built in just 30 years (1151-1174), giving it remarkable stylistic unity. Its stone-scaled dome, inspired by Byzantine models, is unique in the peninsula. Inside, the cathedral museum displays a collection of 15th-century Flemish tapestries.

Tour the Romanesque churches descending toward the Duero: Santa María Magdalena (12th century, richly carved south portal), San Claudio de Olivares (the oldest, 11th century, historiated capitals), Santo Tomé (archivolted windows), San Cipriano (façade bas-reliefs). Each church has its personality—you don't need to be a specialist to appreciate the variety of portals and capitals. Allow 2 hours for a walking route covering the main ones.

Zamora's walls, erected between the 11th and 13th centuries, preserve several monumental gates. The Puerta de Doña Urraca recalls the infanta who defended the city against her brother Sancho II in 1072. The Puerta del Obispo leads directly to the cathedral. The castle, at the tip of the rocky spur, overlooks the Duero—the view from the ramparts embraces the medieval stone bridge and the Castilian plain.

activities.text: If you're in Zamora during Holy Week, you'll witness one of Spain's most impressive celebrations—declared of International Tourist Interest. Processions accompanied by Gregorian chants traverse the medieval streets in an intense atmosphere. The rest of the year, sample local specialties: Zamorano DO cheese, Aliste veal, and wines from DO Toro or DO Arribes.

practical_info.tip: The area around Plaza Mayor has the best traditional restaurants

map_keywords: Castillo de la Mota Medina del Campo
Palacio Real Testamentario Medina del Campo
Plaza Mayor de la Hispanidad Medina del Campo
Colegiata de San Antolín Medina del Campo
Museo de las Ferias Medina del Campo

visits.text: Drive east to Medina del Campo in the heart of the Castilian plain. The Castillo de la Mota greets you from afar—it's one of Castile's largest castles. Built in the 15th century by John II and completed by the Catholic Monarchs in 1483, it presents a trapezoidal plan with a 40-meter-high keep flanked by four crenellated towers. The castle served successively as royal archives, state prison—César Borgia was imprisoned here in 1506—and residence of Joanna the Mad. You can visit the parade ground, chapel, dungeons, and Queen's mirador—allow 1h30 with the guided tour.

Descend to the Plaza Mayor de la Hispanidad, one of Spain's largest. This is where the international fairs that made Medina's fortune in the 15th and 16th centuries were held—merchants from Portugal, Italy, and Flanders negotiated wool, textiles, and bills of exchange. On one corner of the square stands the Palacio Real Testamentario: it was in this palace that Isabella the Catholic dictated her will in October 1504 and died on November 26 following. The interpretation center recounts her life and her connection to the city.

Visit the Museo de las Ferias, housed in the former church of San Martín (16th century). This museum traces the history of the medieval fairs that made Medina the commercial capital of Castile. The pieces on display—bills of exchange, weights and measures, coins—tell how this small town became one of 15th-century Europe's most important financial centers.

The Collegiate Church of San Antolín, a Gothic-Renaissance building from the 16th-17th centuries, merits a stop for its richly decorated chapels. Then stroll the streets to spot Renaissance palaces built by bankers and merchants from the fair era: the Palacio de Dueñas, Casa Blanca, and Hospital de Simón Ruiz.

activities.text: Medina del Campo hides beneath its streets a network of medieval underground cellars, once used to store wine. Operators organize guided tours of three cellars with different characteristics—allow 1h30 with a tasting. It's an original way to discover the town's underground heritage.

practical_info.tip: Booking required. Minimum 8 people—check with the tourist office for tour dates

visits.text: Arriving in Peñafiel, the castle appears first—impossible to miss. Perched on a narrow ridge between the Duero and Duratón valleys, it hugs the terrain so perfectly it's nicknamed el barco de Castilla (Castile's ship). At 210 meters long but only 33 meters wide, it does indeed resemble a stone vessel sailing the plateau. The earliest mentions date to 943; the current form dates from the 15th century when Pedro Téllez Girón remodeled it in Gothic style. Climb the central keep for a 360° panorama over the Ribera del Duero.

In the castle's south courtyard, the Museo Provincial del Vino traces the entire history of wine in the Duero valley—from vine to glass, covering grape varieties, vinification techniques, and barrel aging. The museum is one of Castile and León's most visited. You finish with a guided tasting of Ribera del Duero wines. Allow 1h30 for the museum and tasting.

Descend into town to discover the Plaza del Coso, an oval square unique in Spain with its painted wooden balconies. Since the 15th century, it has served as a bullring during festivals—bulls run through this enclosed space while spectators watch from the balconies. Just beside it, the convent of San Pablo, founded by the infant Don Juan Manuel in the 14th century—one of medieval Castile's greatest writers—preserves a fine cloister and a collection of sacred art.

activities.text: Peñafiel is the capital of Ribera del Duero, one of Spain's most prestigious wine appellations. The king grape is Tempranillo (called Tinto Fino here), which produces powerful, elegant reds. Operators offer cellar visits with tastings in the surrounding area—the Ribera del Duero wine route has over 300 cellars. The Pintia archaeological site, 4 km away, proves wine was made here 2,500 years ago.

practical_info.tip: Book cellar visits in advance. The wine route has been certified by ACEVIN since 2019

visits.text: Lerma appears in the distance like an ocean liner perched on a hill. The Palacio Ducal dominates the Plaza Ducal—one of Spain's largest squares at 6,862 m². This 17th-century palace, built between 1601 and 1617 by architect Francisco de Mora, is the work of the Duke of Lerma, favorite of Philip III and the most powerful man in Spain at the time. The duke, so influential he dared add four towers to his palace (a privilege normally reserved for kings), made Lerma a parallel court with six monasteries, a collegiate church, and secret passages connecting everything. Its austere façade has 210 balconies and 35 windows. You can visit the cloister and covered inner courtyard.

Take the Pasadizo del Duque, the covered passage the duke built to attend Mass without stepping into the street. It connected the palace to the Collegiate Church of San Pedro (early 17th century), an austere temple whose interior preserves a gilded bronze praying statue of Archbishop Cristóbal de Rojas, the duke's uncle. From the Mirador de los Arcos at the end of the passage, the view plunges over the vega of the Arlanza—the agricultural plain stretching as far as the eye can see.

Cross through the Arco de la Cárcel, the only preserved medieval gate from the old walls. It now houses the headquarters of the DO Arlanza regulatory council. Around the square, visit the convents: San Blas (where Dominican nuns sell hand-painted ceramics), La Ascensión (where the infanta Margarita, daughter of Philip III, was baptized), Santa Teresa (now city hall and interpretation center). Lerma had six monasteries in the 17th century—a village of 600 inhabitants surrounded by convents, that's Habsburg Castile.

activities.text: Use the afternoon for an excursion to Santo Domingo de Silos, 35 km southeast of Lerma. The Benedictine monastery, founded in the 10th century, houses an 11th-century Romanesque cloister considered one of Europe's finest. The carved capitals and corner pillar bas-reliefs are exceptionally refined. The monks still chant Gregorian chant at services.

practical_info.tip: Check service times—the monks' Gregorian chant is a unique experience

visits.text: Long drive from Lerma (2h30 via Burgos and Soria). You leave Duero Castile to enter Guadalajara province. Sigüenza, at nearly 1,000 meters elevation, is one of Spain's best-preserved medieval towns. The cathedral, begun in 1130, combines Romanesque and Gothic. Its massive façade, flanked by two towers, gives it a fortress-like appearance—no accident, as it was built just after the Reconquest. Inside, don't miss the tomb of El Doncel (the page): this funerary sculpture of Martín Vázquez de Arce, who died at 25 in 1486 during the capture of Granada, is considered one of Spanish Gothic's finest. The young man is depicted reclining, reading a book, legs crossed—striking realism for the period.

The castle dominates the town from its rocky outcrop—its origins trace to an 8th-century Arab citadel, itself built on Roman foundations. Reconquered in 1124 by Bishop Bernard of Agen, it became the residence of the town's bishop-lords. In the 15th century, Cardinal Mendoza transformed it into a palace. Ferdinand and Isabella stayed here. The paved courtyard, 13th-century Romanesque chapel, and views over the town from the ramparts justify the climb.

Descend to the Plaza Mayor, opened in the 15th century in front of the cathedral's south door. Its two stone-arcaded sides frame Renaissance houses with carved coats of arms. Wander the medieval quarter: the Casa del Doncel (14th century), residence of the Vázquez de Arce family, presents a handsome crenellated façade. The Romanesque church of San Vicente (12th century) and vestiges of the medieval walls complete the picture.

activities.text: If you enjoy hiking, the Barranco del Río Dulce Natural Park lies a few kilometers from Sigüenza. This canyon carved by the Dulce River through limestone offers landscapes of cliffs, seasonal waterfalls, and rich biodiversity—griffon vultures, golden eagles. The trail between Pelegrina and La Cabrera is the most accessible. Allow 2 to 3 hours for a loop.

practical_info.tip: The village of Pelegrina, with its ruined castle above the canyon, is an unmissable photo stop

map_keywords: Castillo de la Piedra Bermeja Brihuega

visits.text: Brihuega is nicknamed the "Garden of Alcarria"—and in July, "Spanish Provence." This fortified medieval village, declared a historic ensemble in 1973, overlooks the Tajuña valley from its hill. Begin with the 12th-century ramparts, nearly two kilometers long, pierced by monumental gates: the Puerta de la Cadena, Puerta del Juego de Pelota, and Arco de Cozagón. The Castillo de la Piedra Bermeja, south of the village, dates from the same era and offers a panoramic view over the surrounding fields.

Descend into the Cuevas Árabes, a network of underground galleries carved beneath the village. These cellars once served as water reserves and refuges. The cool atmosphere and stone vaults contrast with the Meseta's summer heat. The guided tour lasts about 30 minutes.

The Real Fábrica de Paños is Brihuega's emblematic civil monument. This royal cloth factory, founded by Ferdinand VI in 1750, was given to the inhabitants as reward for their support during the War of Succession. The 18th-century building, with its characteristic rotunda, is a rare example of industrial architecture from this period in Spain. The gardens surrounding it, laid out in 1813 in a geometric style inspired by Versailles, with cypresses, clipped boxwood, and fountains, justify the town's nickname. Writer Camilo José Cela described them as "a romantic garden, a garden to die of adolescent love."

activities.text: If visiting in July, the lavender fields surrounding Brihuega are in full bloom—over 1,000 hectares of plantations representing 10% of global lavender essential oil production. The spectacle of violet expanses stretching to the horizon is breathtaking. Operators offer guided tours of the fields and local distillery. The rest of the year, Alcarria's hills remain beautiful with their fields of cereals, olive trees, and aromatic plants.
