itineraries.source_url: https://whc.unesco.org/fr/list/381/, https://whc.unesco.org/fr/list/316/, https://whc.unesco.org/fr/list/803/, https://whc.unesco.org/fr/list/311/, https://whc.unesco.org/fr/list/348/, https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parc_naturel_d'Arribes_du_Duero, https://www.spain.info/fr/decouvrir-espagne/arribes-duero/, https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cath%C3%A9drale_de_Zamora, https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Las_M%C3%A9dulas, https://www.turismocastillayleon.com/fr/patrimoine-culture/ensemble-ethnologique/penalba-santiago, https://astorga.co/fr/escapade-dun-week-end-sept-choses-a-voir-a-astorga/, https://www.dosde.com/discover/fr/palais-episcopal-dastorga/, https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cath%C3%A9drale_Sainte-Marie_de_Burgos, https://turismoburgos.org/cascada-de-orbaneja-del-castillo/, https://all-andorra.com/fr/frias/, https://tourtravelandmore.com/fr/puentedey-burgos-la-plus-belle-ville-nouvelle-despagne/, https://www.turismocastillayleon.com/fr/nature/paysage-protege-monument-naturel-las-tuerces, https://www.spain.info/fr/destination/soria/, https://www.spain.info/fr/curiosites/monastere-san-juan-duero/, https://www.randos-passion.com/la-laguna-negra-et-le-pico-de-urbion/, https://www.espagnefascinante.fr/articulo/que-voir-castille-et-leon/que-voir-a-calatanazor/, https://www.spain.info/fr/decouvrir-espagne/segovie-monuments-espagne/, https://thatbackpacker.com/discovering-pedraza-spain/, https://whc.unesco.org/fr/list/348/, https://www.randos-passion.com/la-sierra-de-gredos/
itineraries.title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks
itineraries.subtitle: From Salamanca to the Sierra de Gredos, a grand tour through medieval treasures, wild gorges and high-mountain landscapes across Spain's largest autonomous community.
specialties: Cocido maragato (served backwards: meat first, broth last)
Lechazo asado (roast suckling lamb from wood-fired ovens)
Jamón de Guijuelo (cured mountain ham from Salamanca)

visits.text: Salamanca's Plaza Mayor is often called Spain's most beautiful square—and once you see it, you'll understand why. Commissioned by Philip V in 1710, built from 1729 by Alberto de Churriguera and completed only in 1755, it's a masterpiece of Spanish Baroque framed by two-story arcades. Look for the sculpted medallions on the arches: you'll find kings, conquistadors, and even Franco—though he's since been removed following historical memory legislation. At the corner location, Café Novelty has been serving since 1905; it's the perfect spot for a break with a view. Arrive early morning or after 8pm for local atmosphere, as school groups flood the square during the day.

Salamanca's twin cathedrals are joined together and visited as one. The Catedral Vieja, begun around 1150 in Romanesque style, houses one of Spain's most complete collections of medieval altarpieces—53 painted panels from the 15th century. The Catedral Nueva, built between the 16th and 18th centuries in late Gothic style, is notably more imposing. On the north facade, restored in 1992, look for an astronaut and a demon eating ice cream—added according to the tradition that each restoration should include a contemporary figure. You enter the old cathedral through the new one, with a single combined ticket.

The facade of Salamanca's University, founded in 1218 by Alfonso IX of León—one of Europe's oldest—is covered with plateresque carvings so dense they're sometimes compared to stone lace. The classic game: find the hidden frog on the facade. Legend says whoever finds it will pass their exams. The interior, notably the old lecture hall called Aula de Fray Luis de León, is open for visits and worth the stop.

The Casa de las Conchas is a 15th-century Gothic civil palace whose facade is covered with over 350 stone scallop shells—the emblem of the Order of Santiago to which its owner, Rodrigo Arias Maldonado, belonged. Now a public library, entry is free and the interior courtyard is worth five minutes of your time.

activities.text: Late in the day, walk down to the Puente Romano and the riverbank viewpoint along the Tormes to watch the silhouette of both cathedrals at sunset. It's the best photo angle in Salamanca. From the Roman bridge—26 arches, built under Trajan, the city's only access point for two thousand years—the orange light on Villamayor stone is the spectacle that earned the city its nickname 'La Dorada' (the Golden One).

map_keywords: Mirador del Fraile Aldeadávila de la Ribera

visits.text: The Arribes del Duero Natural Park stretches for nearly 100 kilometers of canyons carved by the Duero River through granite, forming the natural border between Spain and Portugal. The cliffs plunge up to 500 meters—the deepest gorges in the entire Iberian Peninsula. The Mirador del Fraile, at Aldeadávila de la Ribera, offers the most vertiginous viewpoint: a walkway overhangs the canyon directly, not recommended if you have a fear of heights. From there, the Duero's waters seem motionless, they're so far below. Bring water as the trails are exposed.

The Pozo de los Humos is one of the peninsula's most spectacular waterfalls: the Uces River plunges into the canyon from over 50 meters, creating a permanent cloud of spray that gave it its name ('well of smoke'). The trail from Masueco is short (2.3 km round trip) and accessible to all, but stones are slippery in wet season. In summer, flow can be low depending on the year's rainfall—choose spring or autumn to see the falls at full force.

Make a detour through Fermoselle, a town declared a historic and artistic site, perched on the heights with views over the confluence of the Duero and Esla. The town is nicknamed 'the town of a thousand cellars'—caves carved into the rock beneath houses to store wine from the Arribes terroir, a developing denomination of origin with eleven vineyards planted on terraces. The viewpoints at the castle and Las Escaleras are worth the stop.

activities.text: Walk sections of the GR-14, the long-distance trail that follows the Duero from its source to Porto. In the Arribes sector, several sections above the gorges are accessible in 1 to 3 hours. The microclimate warmer than the rest of Castile—due to the deep gorges trapping heat—allows olive trees, almonds and vines to grow at this latitude. Look up: golden eagles, griffon vultures and black storks nest on the rock walls.

map_keywords: Iglesia Santiago de los Caballeros Zamora

visits.text: Zamora's cathedral is nicknamed 'The Pearl of the Duero'—and once you see its scaled stone dome, you'll understand why. Built between 1151 and 1174, consecrated in 1175, it's the oldest cathedral in Castile and León. Its cimborrio (lantern tower) with sixteen double arches showing Byzantine, Islamic and French Romanesque influences is a unique creation in Spanish Romanesque art. Inside, don't miss the Cathedral Museum which houses one of the world's finest collections of 15th-century Flemish tapestries, from the workshops of Arras, Tournai and Brussels. Check opening times before coming, as the cathedral closes during certain exhibition periods.

Zamora has over twenty Romanesque churches in the old town—making it the densest concentration in Europe according to several art historians. Follow the Romanesque circuit from Plaza de Viriato: the church of La Magdalena, with its carved doorway and oriental sepulcher, then Santiago de los Caballeros, where tradition places El Cid's knighting by Ferdinand I. The Mirador del Troncoso, at the end of the ramparts, gives panoramic views over the Duero and the plains of Castile.

Zamora's ramparts extend over a kilometer along the rocky promontory overlooking the Duero. Built in several phases from the 9th century onward, they're among the best preserved in Spain. Look for the Portillo de la Traición—a small gate in the wall through which, according to legend, the assassin of King Sancho II was introduced during the siege of Zamora in 1072. This is the origin of the Spanish proverb 'Zamora wasn't taken in an hour.'

activities.text: Late afternoon, head down to Playa de Los Pelambres on the Duero's banks to view the silhouette of the cathedral and castle from the opposite shore. The medieval stone bridge, crossing point of the Via de la Plata—the Roman road that linked Mérida to Astorga—holds one of Spain's oldest sets of aceñas (water mills). This is the best angle for photographing the historic ensemble.

map_keywords: Cueva de La Cuevona Las Médulas

visits.text: Las Médulas is Castile and León's strangest landscape: reddish sand pinnacles rise from a forest of oaks and chestnuts, like a fantasy film set. This isn't natural geology—it's the direct result of the Roman Empire's largest open-pit gold mine. The Romans extracted approximately 800,000 kilos of gold here between the 1st and 3rd centuries, using a process called 'ruina montium': they stored colossal amounts of water in mountain basins, then released it all at once to explode the rock. Over 400 km of canals and aqueducts were built to feed this system. Listed as UNESCO in 1997, the site has remained intact since the mines were abandoned. Start at the Interpretation Center in the village to understand the operation's scale before heading out on the trails.

Climb up to the Mirador de Orellán for the overview of the site—from here the spectacle is most striking: the jagged red formations stand out against the green forests, with the Aquilanos mountains in the background. The sunset from this viewpoint is one of the most photographed in León province. Allow 30 minutes uphill from the Orellán parking lot.

activities.text: Walk the Senda de las Valiñas, a circular route from Las Médulas village that penetrates the heart of the mining site. The trail crosses the open-air galleries, skirts Lake Carucedo (formed by stagnant water accumulation during Roman works) and passes through the caves of La Cuevona and La Encantada—galleries hand-dug for the hydraulic system. Easy, marked, about 2 hours. If you have more time, the Peripheral Trail (4h) makes the complete circuit and includes the ruins of the Orellán settlement.

map_keywords: Monasterio San Pedro de Montes Bierzo

visits.text: To reach Peñalba de Santiago, you must cross the entire Valley of Silence by a single-lane road once called 'the monks' path.' Perched at 1,100 meters in the Aquilanos mountains, this village of about fifty inhabitants is one of Spain's most beautiful: stone and slate houses with wooden balconies, lanes so narrow they seem never to have changed since the 10th century. Park at the entrance lot—the lanes are impassable by car.

The Mozarabic church of Santiago de Peñalba is the only remnant of the monastery founded by San Genadio in the 10th century, completed in 937 by Abbot Salomón. It's a jewel of Spanish Mozarabic architecture—a style born from the fusion of Visigothic traditions and Arab influences. Enter through the double door with horseshoe arches supported by three marble columns: inside, the horseshoe arches divide the nave, and remnants of medieval wall paintings survive on the walls. This is where the body of San Genadio rests.

From the village, a half-hour walk on the old monks' path leads to the Cave of San Genadio, carved into a vertical rock face at the very entrance to the Valley of Silence. The Bishop of Astorga retreated here to meditate in the 9th century. A local legend says he ordered the Oza River to be silent so as not to disturb his prayer—and the river disappeared underground at that spot.

activities.text: The Valley of Silence is also a beautiful invitation to hike in a landscape of oak, chestnut and walnut forests crossed by the Oza River. The trail climbing to the Monastery of San Pedro de Montes (founded in the 7th century by San Fructuoso, rebuilt in the 12th-13th centuries) is one of the region's most beautiful. Allow 3 hours round trip from Peñalba.

visits.text: The Episcopal Palace of Astorga is one of Gaudí's few works outside Catalonia—and it's as surprising as any of his Barcelona buildings. After the old palace burned down in 1886, Bishop Joan Grau i Vallespinós, originally from Catalonia and Gaudí's childhood friend, commissioned him to build the new one. From 1887, Gaudí designed a neo-Gothic edifice in white granite combining the forms of a castle, temple and manor house. The moat, battlements, four circular towers and multicolored stained glass windows—inspired by León Cathedral—bear his signature. Bishop Grau's death in 1893 prompted Gaudí to abandon the site, with the top floor completed without him by architect Ricardo García Guereta. Today the palace houses the Museum of the Ways of Saint James. Book tickets online; audio guides are available in English.

Astorga Cathedral adjoins Gaudí's palace, forming a striking ensemble. Begun in 1471 on the site of an earlier Romanesque cathedral, it blends Gothic, Renaissance and Baroque according to the centuries of construction. Its Baroque facade is particularly ornate. On an apse pinnacle, spot the statue of Pedro Mato, a legendary figure of the Maragatería whose identity remains mysterious. Inside, the main altarpiece and carved choir are worth stopping for.

Astorga is a Roman town founded under Augustus as Asturica Augusta, and the remains are remarkably well preserved. The Roman Museum, accessed through the Ergastula (2nd-century Roman dungeon still standing), displays discoveries from local excavations. The baths, forum and Domus of the Bear and Birds—with its Roman mosaics in place—complete the visit. The city was the hub of several major Roman roads, including the one linking Bordeaux to Astorga. This crossroads also explains its role as an essential stop on the Way of St. James.

activities.text: Five kilometers from Astorga, the village of Castrillo de los Polvazares is the best-preserved example of Maragato architecture—reddish stone houses aligned on a single paved street, carriage gates, absolute silence. The Maragatería is a unique region whose inhabitants, the Maragatos, had a reputation as muleteer-transporters of goods between Galicia and Castile. Their traditional dish, cocido maragato, is served backwards from other Spanish cocidos: meat first, broth last.

visits.text: Burgos Cathedral is the only religious building in Spain inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list independently. The first stone was laid by Ferdinand III and Bishop Mauricio on July 20, 1221; construction stretched until 1765, summarizing in itself the entire history of Spanish Gothic. Its 84-meter towers topped with openwork spires from the 15th century dominate the old town for miles. Inside, look for the tomb of El Cid and his wife Doña Jimena beneath the central lantern tower. Don't miss the Chapel of the Constables (Capilla del Condestable), a masterpiece of late Gothic, or the Papamoscas that chimes the hours from a balcony between two bells. Arrive late afternoon from Astorga: the illuminated cathedral at dusk is a spectacle in itself. Book tickets online.

The next morning before leaving Burgos, don't miss the Cartuja de Miraflores, 3 km from the center. This 15th-century Gothic monastery houses the polychrome tomb of the parents of the Catholic Monarchs—John II and Isabella of Portugal—sculpted by Gil de Siloé. It's one of the summits of Spanish Gothic sculpture. Entry is free. From the forecourt, the Arco de Santa María and the Paseo del Espolón along the Arlanzón River offer a beautiful transitional walk before the drive to Orbaneja.

activities.text: The Museum of Human Evolution is directly linked to excavations at the Atapuerca site, 15 km from Burgos, where the oldest human remains in Western Europe were discovered—Homo antecessor, approximately 800,000 years old. The original collections and reconstructions make for a fascinating half-day even for non-specialists. If time is tight, prioritize the cathedral on arrival evening and the Cartuja on departure morning.

map_keywords: Cueva del Agua Orbaneja del Castillo

visits.text: Orbaneja del Castillo is perhaps Spain's most improbable village: a 25-meter waterfall emerges directly from the Cueva del Agua, flows through the village dividing houses into two quarters, and plunges into the Ebro below. Along the way, the water has formed terraced pools with turquoise reflections surrounded by green moss—the contrast is striking. The village, declared a historic site in 1993, has about fifty inhabitants and stone lanes where time seems frozen. Park at the lot outside the center.

The Cueva del Agua, from which the waterfall springs, is a karst cave you can visit guided in summer and on weekends. From the upper part of the town, climb to Orbaneja's natural viewpoint for a plunging view over the Ebro canyon—the limestone walls cut out rock silhouettes that locals compare to African profiles. Also look for the natural arch that the river has sculpted in the cliff above the village.

activities.text: The Ebro canyon (Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón) offers spectacular hikes from Orbaneja. The circular route linking Orbaneja del Castillo to Valdelateja follows the canyon for 17 km—allow a good half-day. If you prefer something shorter, the GR-99 between Pesquera de Ebro and Manzanedo offers the best gorge views in 2-3 hours.

visits.text: Frías is considered Spain's smallest 'town'—it received this title in 1435 and today has only about a hundred inhabitants, a member of Spain's Most Beautiful Villages since 2014. From the bottom of the Tobalina valley, the castle stands atop the limestone outcrop called La Muela: built between the 12th and 15th centuries on Alfonso VIII's orders, it contains 13th-century Romanesque capitals depicting warriors on horseback and fantastic birds. The panoramic view over the valley from the keep is worth the climb. Buy the ticket at the tourist office before heading up.

Along the slope below the castle, Frías's medieval lanes reveal the 'hanging houses'—half-timbered constructions clinging to the cliff edge, upper floors jutting out into space above the gorge. Descend to the medieval bridge over the Ebro (143 meters, 9 arches) with its 14th-century defense tower. The San Vicente church is also notable: its original Romanesque portal was detached and shipped to New York when the tower collapsed in 1904—it's now in an American museum.

Forty-six kilometers from Frías, Puentedey takes its name from its geology: the 50-inhabitant village is built on a natural limestone bridge sculpted by the Nela River over 85 to 90 million years. It's the most photographed image in Burgos province after the cathedral. From the suspended viewpoint of San Andrés, the overall view of the natural bridge and the houses perched on it is striking. On the arch itself, the San Pelayo church (11th-century Romanesque elements) and the Brizuela Palace (15th century, two rectangular towers with coats of arms) merit a stop.

activities.text: Walk the cobbled lanes climbing toward the castle. The medieval structure of the town can be read in the tangle of passages and stairs.
Go down to the bridge to photograph Frías from the opposite bank. The silhouette of the village and its castle stands out against the sky.

visits.text: The Las Tuerces labyrinth resembles a ghost city sculpted by water: for millions of years, karst erosion on Upper Cretaceous limestone has created a tangle of giant stone mushrooms, natural arches, blind corridors and shaded galleries. You feel like you're on another planet—with free access and no queues. The site is accessed from the village of Villaescusa de las Torres. Set off without a fixed route, explore paths at random.

activities.text: At the labyrinth's foot, the Cañón de la Horadada is a gorge carved by the Pisuerga River with vertical walls over 40 meters. The trail from Mave descends into the gorge along the old greenway, passes the former hydroelectric plant and reaches a rock wall allowing you to climb back up for the overview. The combination Las Tuerces + Cañón de la Horadada in a loop from Aguilar de Campoo makes 9.5 km and about 2h30. Site included in the UNESCO Global Geopark 'Las Loras'.

map_keywords: Pico de Urbión Sierra de Urbión

visits.text: Laguna Negra is one of Soria province's most impressive natural destinations: a glacial lake nestled at 1,750 meters altitude, encircled by granite walls and pine forests. The name comes from the dark color of its waters, reflecting the cliffs surrounding it. The Urbión Glacial Lagoons and Cirques Natural Park, created in 2010 over 4,617 hectares, protects this ensemble. From the Paso de la Serrá parking (mandatory access in high season), the paved path leads in a few minutes to a wooden walkway at the lake's edge.

activities.text: The loop hike from Paso de la Serrá to Pico de Urbión (2,228 m) via Laguna Negra and Laguna Helada is one of the most beautiful in the entire Iberian System. Allow 12 km and about 4 hours with 664 m of elevation gain—rated difficult on AllTrails with a 4.6/5 score. From the summit, you'll see the plains of Castile to the south, La Rioja and the Ebro to the north. This summit is also where the Duero River is born. Wear high-top shoes: the rocky corridor above the lake is steep. In winter and spring, hard snow may be present.

map_keywords: Monasterio San Juan de Duero Soria

visits.text: The cloister of San Juan de Duero Monastery is one of Spain's strangest—and that's precisely what makes it unforgettable. Built in the 13th century by the Order of the Hospitallers of Jerusalem on the banks of the Duero, its four galleries each present a different arch style: Romanesque, Gothic, Islamic and interlaced horseshoe arches with no equivalent in the West. Some are inspired by Andalusian mosques, others by the Crusades. This unique architectural syncretism reflects exactly medieval Spain at the crossroads of cultures. Entry is free for EU citizens. On leaving, walk along the Duero to the Hermitage of San Saturio, carved into the rock in the 18th century over older caves—the Baroque interior is a surprise in this rock-hewn setting.

In Soria's historic center, the Co-Cathedral of San Pedro preserves a 12th-century Romanesque cloister with capitals carved with fantastic animals, vegetation and biblical scenes—three of its four original galleries still stand. Tradition has it that the Romanesque tower fell in 1531 because a city councilor asked an architect to remove a pillar that blocked the view of a statue of the Virgin. The Alameda de Cervantes Park, along the Duero, is the city's green lung with over 130 recorded plant species.

activities.text: Eight kilometers from Soria, the ruins of Numancia are a place steeped in history: here the Celtiberians heroically resisted the siege by Scipio Aemilianus's Roman legions for 14 months, before choosing collective death over surrender in 133 BC. The site on Cerro de la Muela features house reconstructions and an archaeological route. The Numantine Museum in town gathers objects found at the site.

map_keywords: Casa del Parque Sabinar Muriel de la Fuente

visits.text: To optimize the day before heading to Segovia in the afternoon, start at opening time with La Fuentona de Muriel: a karst spring that surges from a rocky amphitheater as if water appears from nowhere. An accessible trail follows the Abión River to the spring, then a fork leads to a waterfall (with water in wet season). Start from the Muriel de la Fuente parking lot, about 7 km round trip, 3 hours with breaks.

Two kilometers from La Fuentona, the Sabinar de Calatañazor Nature Reserve is Europe's densest forest of Spanish junipers (Juniperus thurifera). Some specimens reach 15 meters in height and 5 meters in diameter, with ages potentially exceeding 400 years. The resinous, slightly incense-like scent emanating from these twisted trees is unforgettable. Allow about an hour's walk.

Finish with the village of Calatañazor—'Qal'at al-Nusur' in Arabic, the 'castle of eagles.' Declared a historic and artistic ensemble in 1962, it preserves its arcaded medieval main street, adobe and juniper-beam houses, and castle ruins. History says that here in July 1002 Christian armies inflicted on Arab general Almanzor the wound from which he would die a month later, giving birth to the Spanish saying 'At Calatañazor, Almanzor lost his drum.' Head to Segovia around 1pm.

activities.text: Walk through the juniper forest where centenarian trees create a mystical atmosphere. Thrushes, magpies and crows feed on their berries in winter.
Push on to the natural monument of La Fuentona, a karst rock spring giving birth to the Abión River. Underwater caves open in its depths.

map_keywords: Palacio Real La Granja de San Ildefonso

visits.text: Segovia's aqueduct is the first thing you see entering the city—and you never get used to it. Probably built around AD 50, under emperors Vespasian and Trajan, to bring water from the sierra to the city, it reaches its highest point at Plaza del Azoguejo: 28.10 meters, 167 arches, 20,400 granite blocks joined without any mortar. What fascinates engineers: after 2,000 years, the blocks maintain perfect balance through geometry alone. Walk under the arches, then climb the rampart path to see the aqueduct head-on—it's the best angle.

Segovia's Alcázar sits on a rocky spur at the confluence of two rivers, like a fairy-tale castle. Walt Disney reportedly drew inspiration from it for Snow White's castle. Begun in the 11th century, expanded in the 13th-16th centuries, ravaged by fire in 1862 and rebuilt in an even more spectacular neo-Gothic style. This is where Isabella the Catholic was proclaimed queen in 1474. Climb the 152 steps of the Torre de Juan II for the best view over the old town and Guadarrama sierra.

Segovia's cathedral, nicknamed 'the Lady of Cathedrals,' is the last Gothic cathedral built in Spain. Construction began in 1525 under Charles V to replace the Romanesque cathedral destroyed during the Comuneros revolt in 1520. Inside, the Flemish Gothic cloister comes from the destroyed old cathedral—it was dismantled stone by stone and rebuilt here. Allow about 1h30.

The Judería quarter and its lanes around Calle de la Judería hold traces of the Sephardic community that lived in Segovia until the 1492 expulsion. Don't miss the Casa de los Picos, a 15th-century palace whose facade is entirely covered with granite points—a unique architectural curiosity. The Barrio de los Caballeros groups the Renaissance palaces of Segovian nobility.

activities.text: Twelve kilometers from Segovia, the Royal Palace of La Granja de San Ildefonso is a miniature version of Versailles at the foot of the Guadarrama sierra: built in the 18th century by Philip V, it's surrounded by French-style gardens with 26 monumental fountains fed by gravity. The water displays run at certain times weekly in summer—check times before going, as this spectacle is what makes the trip worthwhile.

visits.text: Pedraza is the best-preserved medieval village in Castile and León—perhaps in all Spain. Declared a historic site in 1951, it has only one access gate (16th-century wood), cobbled lanes lined with houses bearing noble coats of arms, and an arcaded Plaza Mayor ranking among the region's most beautiful. Park at the outside lot and enter on foot. To avoid weekend crowds, prefer a weekday or arrive before 10am.

Pedraza's castle occupies a rocky outcrop at the village's edge. Built in the 13th century, transformed into a Renaissance palace in the 16th-17th centuries, it was saved from ruin by painter Ignacio Zuloaga who made it his residence and studio. Its keep now houses a museum with some of his works. A little-known historical anecdote: the sons of King Francis I of France were imprisoned in this castle in 1526 as hostages to guarantee the Treaty of Madrid—they stayed four years. Book tickets online.

Pedraza's medieval prison (Cárcel de la Villa), a 13th-century building rebuilt in the 16th, is one of Spain's best preserved. It's visited with guided tours that recreate medieval prison life. If you're there on the first or second Saturday evenings of July, don't miss under any circumstances the Noche de las Velas: electric lights are turned off and thousands of candles illuminate the lanes while concerts take place outdoors.

activities.text: Stroll Calle Mayor between blazoned houses and noble 16th-17th century constructions, witnesses to the village's textile prosperity.
Savor cordero asado, wood-oven roasted lamb that made Pedraza's gastronomic reputation. The British Times in 2024 ranked restaurant El Soportal among the 28 best Mediterranean tables.
Attend the Candle Nights on the first two Saturdays of July. Thousands of candles illuminate lanes, balconies and squares while classical music concerts resound.

visits.text: Ávila's wall is quite simply the most complete and best preserved of medieval Spain. Built from 1090 on Alfonso VI's orders by Count Raymond of Burgundy, it draws a regular rectangle of 2,516 meters perimeter, pierced by 9 gates and flanked by 88 semi-circular towers. UNESCO-listed in 1985. Buy the rampart walk ticket (about €5): 1,700 meters of marked route atop the walls with plunging views over the city and sierra. The cathedral's apse, directly integrated into the wall, is one of the world's only examples of a cathedral playing a defensive role.

The intra-muros old town of Ávila deserves to be walked without a fixed route. Stop at the Basilica of San Vicente, a 12th-century Romanesque masterpiece built on the saint's martyrdom site, with an exceptional Gothic cenotaph. Also visit the Royal Monastery of Santo Tomás, commissioned by the Catholic Monarchs in the late 15th century—Grand Inquisitor Tomás de Torquemada is buried there. Plaza del Mercado Chico is the city's daily heart.

activities.text: The Cuatro Postes (Four Columns) viewpoint, 500 meters outside the walls, is the best vantage point for Ávila's wall in its entirety. Four granite columns form the structure—they were reportedly erected at the spot where child Teresa of Ávila, en route to be martyred by the Moors, was intercepted by her uncle. From here photographers capture the wall's silhouette at sunset. Allow 5 minutes by car or 20 minutes on foot from Puerta del Puente.

visits.text: The Sierra de Gredos is Castile and León's wildest massif—and one of Spain's most beautiful. Pico Almanzor, at 2,592 meters, is the highest summit in the Spanish Central System. The starting point for all major hikes is the Plataforma de Gredos, at 1,780 meters altitude, accessible from Hoyos del Espino via the AV-931 (12 km, toll per vehicle at park entrance). In summer and on weekends, the barrier opens at 7:30am—arrive early as parking fills fast. For the return, Ávila is 80 km away (1h15) and Madrid 170 km (2h) via the A-5.

activities.text: The hike to Laguna Grande de Gredos from the Plataforma is the massif's most beautiful and most frequented (4.6/5 rating on AllTrails, 162 reviews). The trail follows the old Calzada Romana, gradually climbing toward the Mirador de los Barrerones (2,149 m) where the glacial cirque suddenly appears—a breathtaking spectacle. The lake is nestled at 1,950 meters in the rocky amphitheater, guarded by Pico Almanzor. Along the way, Iberian ibex—protected in the park since 1905—are frequently visible on rocky slopes. Allow 14 km round trip, 677 m elevation gain, 5-5.5 hours walking. The Refugio Elola at the lake's edge (80 places) allows overnight stays for summit attempts the next morning—reservation mandatory in season.

seo.h1_title: Castile and León: Ramparts, Canyons and Peaks - Complete 17-Day Itinerary
seo.intro_paragraph: From Salamanca's golden stone to Ávila's ramparts, through the abyssal gorges of the Arribes del Duero, the Roman gold mines of Las Médulas, the Valley of Silence, Gaudí's palace in Astorga, the waterfalls of Las Merindades, Segovia's aqueduct and the glacial cirques of the Sierra de Gredos: this 17-day itinerary makes a complete circuit of Castile and León without ever passing through the same place twice.
seo.seo_hook: Gothic cathedrals piercing the sky, stone villages perched on cliffs, gorges carved 500 meters deep, an unexpected Gaudí palace in the heart of León, UNESCO-listed Roman mines. Castile and León is unlike any other region.
featured_list: Salamanca: the golden city and its twin adjoined cathedrals, UNESCO
Las Médulas: the Roman Empire's largest gold mine, UNESCO
Astorga: Gaudí's episcopal palace, rare outside Catalonia
Burgos: Spain's only cathedral individually inscribed on UNESCO
Segovia: a 28-meter Roman aqueduct without mortar in the city heart
Ávila: Spain's most complete medieval walls, UNESCO

places_taglines.ES::salamanca: Where Spain's oldest university meets twin golden cathedrals
places_taglines.ES::arribes-del-duero: Where the Duero carved Spain's deepest canyon into the Portuguese border
places_taglines.ES::zamora: Europe's Romanesque capital where twenty medieval churches crown a single hilltown
places_taglines.ES::las-medulas: Where Roman engineers moved mountains for gold and left an alien landscape
places_taglines.ES::penalba-de-santiago: A tenth-century Mozarabic sanctuary at the end of the monks' path
places_taglines.ES::astorga: Where Gaudí built a neo-Gothic palace for a bishop far from Barcelona
places_taglines.ES::burgos: Where El Cid rests beneath Spain's only solo-listed UNESCO cathedral
places_taglines.ES::orbaneja-del-castillo: Where a waterfall splits the village before plunging into the Ebro
places_taglines.ES::frias: Spain's smallest town where houses hang from cliffs above a medieval bridge
places_taglines.ES::las-tuerces: A limestone labyrinth sculpted by 90 million years of water and wind
places_taglines.ES::soria: Where four architectural traditions met in one cloister and Celtiberians defied Rome
places_taglines.ES::laguna-negra-urbion: Where a glacial lake mirrors granite peaks at the Duero's source
places_taglines.ES::calatanazor: Where Almanzor lost his final battle among Europe's oldest junipers
places_taglines.ES::segovia: Where Romans stacked granite without mortar and Disney found his castle
places_taglines.ES::pedraza: A walled village with one wooden gate and a thousand candles in July
places_taglines.ES::avila: Where 88 towers and 2,516 meters of medieval walls circle a living city
places_taglines.ES::sierra-de-gredos: Where ibex roam glacial cirques beneath Central Spain's highest peak

highlights: Salamanca and its twin adjoined cathedrals, UNESCO-listed
The abyssal gorges of Arribes del Duero at the Portuguese border
Las Médulas, the Roman Empire's largest gold mine, UNESCO
Gaudí's episcopal palace in Astorga, rare outside Catalonia
The impossible villages of Las Merindades: Orbaneja, Frías, Puentedey
Segovia's Roman aqueduct and Alcázar, UNESCO-listed
Ávila's walls, Spain's most complete, UNESCO-listed
The hike to Sierra de Gredos glacial cirque

itineraries.title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route
itineraries.subtitle: From Miranda del Castañar's intact ramparts to the Arribes del Duero cliffs, ten days exploring Salamanca's best-preserved medieval villages.
itineraries.notes: A sweeping arc from the Sierra de Francia to the Portuguese frontier, through villages declared historic sites, 14th-century castles, and landscapes ranging from mountain peaks to river canyons.
specialties: Hornazo (savory pie with ham and eggs)
Limón serrano (cold salad from Sierra de Francia)
Iberian cured meats from the sierra

map_keywords: Sanctuaire Peña de Francia El Cabaco

visits.text: Sequeros, once the administrative capital of Sierra de Francia, sits at 870 meters altitude with three-story houses and wrought-iron balconies that tell the story of a prosperous 19th-century local bourgeoisie. Declared a site of historic interest in 2004, the village counts fewer than 250 inhabitants, yet its center remains remarkably preserved. Don't miss Plaza de Eloy Bullón—this is what's left of a former aljama, the medieval Jewish quarter, with lanes so narrow the rooftops nearly touch. The labyrinthine layout reflects centuries of coexistence among Christian, Jewish, and Muslim communities.

The Church of Virgen del Robledo stands as Sequeros's most symbolic monument. Its baroque altarpiece and mudéjar coffered ceiling at the main altar deserve your attention. In its crypt lie the remains of Simón Vela—the French pilgrim Simon Roland who, according to legend, received an apparition of the Virgin in 1434 directing him to a statue hidden for centuries atop Peña de Francia. When he died in 1438, he was buried here in Sequeros, not on the mountain.

Push up to the summit of Peña de Francia at 1,727 meters—count on 20 minutes' drive from Sequeros. The sanctuary of Notre-Dame-du-Rocher-de-France, run by Dominicans since the 15th century, offers 360-degree views spanning the plains of Salamanca, Portugal, and Extremadura. Cervantes mentioned it in Don Quixote, and the Black Virgin is venerated as far away as Latin America and the Philippines. The road is passable from April to November—avoid other months when the summit can remain snowbound.

activities.text: Walk to the Cruz viewpoint to contemplate the Béjar-Candelario range—the village's finest lookout. The Barrero trail leading there edges past the old circular bullring, now converted to public space. Have a glass of mountain wine in one of the bars on Church Square, beneath the granite arcades.

Come evening, explore the Robledo trail departing from the village—a marked 2-3 km path crossing oak forests and reaching the hermitage of Robledo where rest the bones of Juana, 'la Moza Santa,' Sequeros's local prophetess whose legend is intimately bound to the discovery of the Virgin of Peña de Francia.

map_keywords: Château San Vicente Montemayor del Río
Église Nuestra Señora de la Asunción Montemayor del Río

visits.text: Montemayor del Río occupies a spectacular enclave in the Sierra de Béjar, at the historic frontier between the kingdoms of León, Castile, and Extremadura. Its San Vicente castle commands a precious valley planted with chestnut and oak. The fortress was built in the late 13th century on orders of Infante Don Pedro, son of Alfonso X the Wise—but it's the 14th and 15th centuries that gave the castle its present appearance, with four towers (two round, two square) and its keep. In 1538, Charles V created the Marquessate of Montemayor for Juan de Silva, Captain General of Navarre. Declared a Site of Cultural Interest in 1949, the castle now houses a Medieval Interpretation Center with guided tours.

The village itself deserves a long wander. Its medieval streets, church, and old center lanes hosted merchants and muleteers for centuries on the Cañada Real (old transhumance route) and the Calzada—the Roman Via de la Plata linking Mérida to Astorga. The arcades on the plaza and carved lintels bear witness to a town of passage and exchange.

activities.text: In summer, Montemayor del Río's natural pools—carved into granite and fed by a mountain stream—rank among the region's finest. Water flows straight down from the Sierra de Béjar peaks. Count on a half-hour walk from the village to reach them.

Lunch in Montemayor offers a chance to try the stews and mountain sausages typical of the Sierra de Béjar—quality cured meats, some prepared in villagers' own homes. Nearby in Béjar, you'll also find local specialties featuring Iberian ham.

visits.text: Arriving in Mogarraz, you'll sense you're being watched—and it's no illusion. Over 800 portraits of former residents gaze down from every house façade in the village. The story behind this gallery is striking: in 1967, a local photographer took ID photos of the 388 inhabitants so they could obtain identity cards without traveling to Salamanca. Those negatives slept in a cigar box for decades until artist Florencio Maíllo—born in the village—rediscovered them and decided in 2012 to paint each face on metal plates and hang them on the façade where the person had lived. The exhibition, called 'Retrata2-388,' was meant to last six months—villagers refused to remove the portraits, and the project has only grown since.

The Hermitage of Humilladero, a small 13th-century temple at the village entrance, marks the start of the visit. Just in front, the crucero de los judíos (Jews' cross from the 17th century) bears carved skulls recalling the village's past as a converted Jewish quarter—Mogarraz was founded and repopulated in the 11th century by French and Gascon settlers according to historical sources, and its historic center is classified as a Site of Cultural Interest.

The Ethnographic Museum Casa de las Artesanías, on the main square, presents Sierra de Francia traditions and crafts: embroidery, leatherwork, filigree in silver and gold. It's a useful complement for understanding the human context behind the portraits on the façades.

activities.text: Don't leave without doing the Camino del Agua—a marked 6 km loop departing from the village through chestnut and oak forests, with waterfalls and ruined mills. It's one of the trails in the 'Arte en la Naturaleza' series developed by Salamanca province: you'll encounter contemporary art installations in the midst of nature. Count on 2½ hours at an easy pace.

At the table, don't leave without trying limón serrano—a cold salad typical of Sierra de Francia blending citrus, cured meat, hard-boiled egg, and canned fish, originating with the converted Jews who inhabited the region and composed their dishes with whatever they had on hand.

map_keywords: Église Nuestra Señora de la Asunción La Alberca
Casa Museo Satur Juanela La Alberca

visits.text: La Alberca became the very first village in all of Spain to be declared a Historic-Artistic Site—in 1940, by decree of September 6, published in the BOE on the 18th of the same month. This pioneering title is well deserved: its granite houses with ground floors topped by upper stories in adobe and timber forming warm half-timbering are the perfect model of serrana architecture. The village name comes from the Arabic 'Al-Bereka,' meaning pond or water place—the numerous fountains dotting the lanes are its legacy. The Plaza Mayor, with its irregular layout, granite arcades, and flower-decked balconies, displays an 18th-century cross carved with symbols of the Passion.

The Church of Nuestra Señora de la Asunción, built in 1730 by Manuel de Larra Churriguera (architect of Salamanca Cathedral), is a baroque jewel in this medieval setting. Its grand tower was erected in 1518 by the first Dukes of Alba—their coat of arms still visible on one corner. Inside, ask to see the Cristo del Sudor, attributed to Juan de Juni, and the 16th-century polychrome wooden altarpiece.

Walk down to the Puente del Arroyo, the old granite bridge spanning the stream below the village—a classic view of serrana architecture. Along the way, you'll pass the Hornacina de las Ánimas, a wall niche where each evening a local woman comes out with her bell (esquila) to recite a prayer for the souls in purgatory. This nighttime tradition is still alive.

The Casa Museo Sátur Juanela, adjacent to the square, preserves the interior of a typical serrana house as it was in the early 20th century—farmyard, wine cellars on the ground floor, kitchen under the eaves. It's the most concrete way to understand how mountain villagers lived.

activities.text: Each year in the village, a pig is released into the streets on June 13 (Saint Anthony's Day) and fed by residents until January 17, when it's raffled off. Proceeds now go to an NGO. If you visit outside this period, you'll still find the statue of the marrano de San Antón at the church corner. This tradition reaches back to the Middle Ages and is one of the most photographed in Spain.

The route into Parque Natural Las Batuecas-Sierra de Francia starts from La Alberca. The Batuecas Path descends into a steep, secret valley where Neolithic cave paintings and a Carmelite monastery survive. Count on a half-day for the round trip to the valley floor.

On the square, sample the obleas and artisan turrones sold in local shops—La Alberca's sweet specialties found throughout the region. Local markets also offer a good opportunity to buy honey, cheese, and sierra cured meats.

map_keywords: Ermita Cristo del Humilladero Miranda del Castañar
Torre de las Campanas Miranda del Castañar
Ermita Virgen de la Cuesta Miranda del Castañar

visits.text: Miranda del Castañar is, along with Ávila, one of only two villages in Spain to have preserved its entire medieval wall circuit intact. The 631 meters of 13th-century fortification encircle the historic core with four gates oriented to the cardinal points. The village sits on a rocky promontory between the Francia and San Benito rivers—its origins trace back to the 12th century with the Hospitaller Order of Jerusalem. In 1213, Alfonso IX of León made it a villa y concejo, administrative capital of Sierra de Francia.

The Zúñiga Castle (14th-15th century) dominates the walled enclosure from its eastern promontory. Its Torre del Homenaje bears an inscription dated 1451 and a Zúñiga coat of arms. The parade ground in front of the castle still serves as a plaza de toros during festivals—locals claim it as Spain's oldest square-shaped bullring. King Enrique IV created the County of Miranda del Castañar in 1457 for his son Diego López de Zúñiga.

The Hermitage of Cristo del Humilladero (13th century) stands at the village entrance, just before you pass through the walls. Here you'll grasp the scale of the defensive system: after the hermitage, a rectangular esplanade that once served as a moat, then the walls and towers. Climb the camino de ronda—the walkway atop the ramparts—for a plunging view over the sierra.

The Bell Tower (Torre de las Campanas) stands separate from the church—deliberately so. It was financed and built in 1612 by the residents so they could ring the bells at will, without depending on the clergy. The tension between municipal power and nobility is readable in every stone of Miranda: more than 80 houses bearing noble coats of arms belonged to great families drawn to the Villa Condal.

activities.text: The Camino de los Prodigios, a 10 km loop linking Miranda del Castañar to Villanueva del Conde through oaks, strawberry trees, and olive trees, is dotted with contemporary art installations integrated into nature. It's the most popular route in the province's 'Arte en la Naturaleza' series. Count on 3½ hours at an easy pace.

In the evening, stroll out to the Hermitage of Virgen de la Cuesta, outside the walls to the south, for the view of Peña de Francia lit by sunset. Legend says the Virgin appeared to children in the hollow of an olive tree at this spot—a 17th-century apparition that remains at the heart of September's patron saint festivals.

map_keywords: Église Nuestra Señora de la Asunción Puente del Congosto

visits.text: Puente del Congosto owes its name and existence to a single feature: the bridge over the Tormes. This strategic crossing on the Cañada Real Soriana Occidental—the great transhumance route descending toward Extremadura—generated enough toll revenue to finance castle, church, and village. The old bridge, built on orders of the Catholic Monarchs around 1500, is solidly founded on granite bedrock and counts 13 arches; it still retains one of its two original defensive towers. Declared a Site of Cultural Interest with historic site status in 2019, it's the last village in the province to receive this title.

The Dávila Castle, on the left bank of the Tormes, was built in the 14th century to defend and control the strategic bridge crossing. In 1393, King Enrique III ceded the lordship of Puente del Congosto to Gil González Dávila, who rebuilt the fortress. In 1539, the Duke of Alba bought it from Charles V and added a second twin tower. Beautifully restored, it's now private property and can only be viewed from outside—but the silhouette from the bridge is remarkable.

Don't leave without walking the riverside path along the Tormes—the crystalline waters and natural swimming holes make Puente del Congosto a popular summer stop. The entire setting, declared a UNESCO biosphere reserve in 2006, is ideal for spotting European bee-eaters and grey herons along the banks.

activities.text: For whitewater enthusiasts, the Tormes upstream from Puente del Congosto offers rafting sections navigable in spring and early summer. Local outfitters offer 2- to 3-hour trips on the river's rapids. Inquire at the tourist office.

Come evening, the menu del día in village bars invariably features hornazo—a savory pie with ham and hard-boiled eggs, a Salamanca specialty you'll find throughout the province. Also try the cured meats from the Sierra de Béjar.

map_keywords: Monastère de l'Annonciation Alba de Tormes
Basilique Sainte Thérèse Alba de Tormes
Musée thérésien CARMUS Alba de Tormes
Église San Juan Alba de Tormes

visits.text: Alba de Tormes is inseparable from Saint Teresa of Ávila. She died here on October 4, 1582, exhausted from a final journey from Burgos, in the Carmelite convent she had founded in 1571. Her body—declared incorruptible at the three tomb openings in 1750, 1914, and 2024—rests in the Church of the Annunciation, in a carved marble urn above the main altar. You can see two reliquaries displaying her heart and left arm. John Paul II came on pilgrimage here November 1, 1982, for the fourth centenary of the saint's death.

The Theresian Basilica, built in 1896 on orders of the Bishop of Salamanca to accommodate the influx of pilgrims, is Alba de Tormes's most visible religious building from the road. Its tower measures 34.6 meters. It's the heart of Marian pilgrimage in Castile and León. The combined visit with the Carmelite Museum (CARMUS)—installed in the convent's renovated cloister—offers the most complete historical and spiritual context on Teresa and John of the Cross.

The Castle of Alba, whose watchtower has guarded the town since the 12th century, symbolizes the Duchy of Alba de Tormes—one of Spain's great noble houses. In 1472, García Álvarez de Toledo obtained the title of Duke of Alba and made Alba a major cultural center of the Golden Age, attracting poets and thinkers. The El Espolón park laid out around the old fortress offers fine views over the Tormes.

The Church of San Juan, built in the 12th-13th centuries, is classified as a National Monument. Its Romanesque-Mudéjar style and the coexistence of brick and stone make it an interesting monument. It preserves the three apses of the original chevet. It's also the first church in the world dedicated to Saint John of the Cross—friend of Teresa of Ávila and co-reformer of the Carmelites.

activities.text: Walk along the banks of the Tormes below the castle—this is where Teresa loved to pray, drawn by the view of the river from her convent. The medieval bridge and old ruined mills on the Tormes compose a calm, photogenic landscape.

At the table, look for rosquillas de Ledesma—sweet doughnuts—and bollo maimón, a soft egg cake found in bakeries throughout Salamanca province. Alba restaurants also serve cabrito (kid goat) and roast lamb.

visits.text: Monleón is one of Salamanca's best-kept secrets. This village of fewer than 100 inhabitants, perched at 900 meters and encircled by medieval walls, barely registers in guidebooks. At the entrance, just beside the puerta de la villa, a zoomorphic granite sculpture—a verraco (bull), typical of the Vettones and dating to the 4th century BC—announces the site's antiquity. The village appears in archives as early as 1199 and was repopulated around 1215.

The 15th-century castle, attached to the walls at the southwest corner of the enclosure, has a wild history. Its owner in 1477, one Don Bernardo Maldonado called 'the Tyrant,' counterfeited money and committed all manner of abuses on neighboring lands. Troops of King Ferdinand the Catholic had to besiege the castle to put him out of commission. The square keep tower still counts 5 levels and 8 turrets.

Outside the walls, on the path leading to the 'Las ollas de la sapa' site, lie the ruins of a 6th-8th century AD medieval village (Monte Alcaide) and several lagares—wine presses carved directly into granite bedrock. These stone basins scattered across the landscape testify to the region's medieval viticulture.

activities.text: The circular hike on GR-181 'Ruta de los Caminos Históricos de Entresierras' between Monleón and El Tornadizo edges along the Alagón River and crosses landscapes of scrubland and granite ravines that are virtually pristine. Count on 3-4 hours depending on your pace for the complete loop.

Monleón has only one bar—an advantage really, since it concentrates all the village's social life. It's the ideal place to talk with the handful of residents and hear the village's history from their mouths. The tapas are renowned for their generosity.

map_keywords: Église Santa María la Mayor Ledesma

visits.text: Ledesma is built on a rocky spur between two meanders of the Tormes—a naturally defensive position inhabited since prehistoric times. The Romans called it Bletisama in honor of Consul Bleto (93 AD). Medieval repopulation dates to 1161, when Fernando II raised the walls and fortress. In 1462, Enrique IV handed lordship of Ledesma to Don Beltrán de la Cueva, his favorite, who made it a city of splendor in the 15th century. Classified as a historic site in 1975, Ledesma preserves fragments of Roman and medieval wall.

Ledesma's castle, partially demolished, has kept what matters: its entrance gate with a Gothic arch of keystones founded directly in bedrock, flanked by a semicircular cube and a polygonal one. It's the most eloquent entrance to the walled enclosure. Inside, the lanes lead to the Gothic church of Santa María la Mayor and the Casa de las Almenas—a 15th-century palace with cloister and interior garden, one of the province's finest noble residences.

The Mocho Bridge, of Roman origin, spans the Tormes at the foot of the cliff on which the town sits. The forest of centuries-old oaks bordering the road to the bridge is a remarkable habitat where you can spot griffon vultures and black kites in flight. A plaque recalls that Mesta flocks crossed this passage for centuries, paying portazgo (toll) to the town's lord.

activities.text: The Mocho Bridge route, marked from the town, crosses a forest of oaks centuries old to reach the Tormes banks. Birdwatchers will easily spot black storks, kites, and vultures from the heights of the riverbank. Allow 1½ hours round trip.

Ledesma is renowned for its thermal mud baths—the aguas termales have been known since Roman times. Wellness establishments still exploit the springs. If you have fatigue from the miles in your legs, it's a welcome break.

map_keywords: Château San Felices de los Gallegos
Muraille médiévale San Felices de los Gallegos
Pont des Français San Felices de los Gallegos
Belvédère Table du Comte San Felices de los Gallegos

visits.text: San Felices de los Gallegos encapsulates the turbulent history of the Spanish-Portuguese frontier. The castle was built in 1296 by King Denis I of Portugal, who had just occupied the town. The annexation by Portugal was confirmed in 1297 at the Treaty of Alcañices. In 1326, San Felices returned to Castilian control as a royal marriage dowry. But that was far from the end of sovereignty exchanges: the town was retaken by the Portuguese during the Castilian War of Succession in the 15th century, then pillaged again during the Portuguese Restoration War in 1646. Its castle is the best-preserved of the four in Arribes del Duero Natural Park.

The Puente de los Franceses (Bridge of the French) spans the Águeda River below the village—the only crossing point of the Arribes de l'Águeda. According to tradition, it was built by Gauls settled in the region, whose name may be the origin of 'Gallegos' in the village name. The descent to the bridge crosses steep slopes of the riverbank with dense Mediterranean vegetation (olive trees, cork oaks) characteristic of the Arribes microclimate.

The Arribes del Duero form one of Spain's most spectacular landscapes: 200-meter-deep canyons carved by the Duero and its tributaries on the Portuguese border. The viewpoint at Mesa del Conde (Table of the Count), accessible from San Felices, offers a vertiginous panorama over the gorges. In 1476, the inhabitants of San Felices revolted against their tyrant lord Gracián de Sessé and hanged him from the tower arch—legend says the shadow of the hanged man stretched all the way to the square.

activities.text: The marked trails of the Arribes del Agueda departing from San Felices offer impressive views of the gorges and colonies of griffon vultures, black storks, and Bonelli's eagles. The avifauna here is among Spain's richest. Choose a morning outing for the best observation conditions.

practical_info.note: Opt for trails marked from the village tourist office

activities.text: The AOC Arribes del Duero wines, produced from centuries-old vines on terraces exposed to the gorges' Mediterranean microclimate, are a discovery for many visitors. The heat reflected from the rock walls produces powerful reds based on Juan García and Rufete. A visit to one of the local estates is a must.

visits.text: Return to Salamanca to close the circuit—the city has an airport and railway station with direct links to Madrid. Salamanca's Plaza Mayor, built between 1729 and 1756 in Churrigueresque style by Alberto de Churriguera, is regularly cited as Spain's most beautiful square. Its golden arcades open onto cafés and restaurants where it's pleasant to pause after ten days of medieval villages.

The Plateresque façade of the University of Salamanca (founded in 1218 by Alfonso IX, one of Europe's oldest) conceals a frog carved in stone. Legend says any student who finds it on their own will pass the year. Search for it without help—you have a one-in-ten chance of remembering. The Roman bridge, built in the 1st century AD on the Via de la Plata, counts 26 arches and measures 176 meters.

activities.text: One last walk along the Tormes banks, at the foot of the Roman bridge, lets you see Salamanca's skyline from the south bank—the same view travelers ascending the Via de la Plata from Extremadura have had for two thousand years. It's a fine endpoint for this circuit.

seo.h1_title: Sierra de Francia Medieval Villages Route
seo.intro_paragraph: La Alberca, Spain's first protected village since 1940. Miranda del Castañar and its 600 meters of intact walls. Mogarraz and its 800 painted portraits on house façades. San Felices de los Gallegos perched above the Duero gorges. Salamanca province harbors a concentration of medieval villages without equal.
seo.seo_hook: Thirteenth-century ramparts still encircle the houses, noble family coats of arms adorn the façades, and portraits painted by a local artist gaze from every wall. These villages have outlasted time—come walk them before nature reclaims its own.
featured_list: La Alberca: Spain's first protected historic site since 1940
Miranda del Castañar: one of only two complete medieval wall circuits in Spain
Mogarraz: 800 portraits of residents painted on house façades
San Felices de los Gallegos: frontier castle between Spain and Portugal

places_taglines.ES::sequeros: Where mountain nobility built balconied houses above narrow medieval lanes
places_taglines.ES::montemayor-del-rio: A fortress on the transhumance route where chestnuts still shade the valley
places_taglines.ES::mogarraz: Eight hundred faces watching from the walls, painted by an artist who came home
places_taglines.ES::la-alberca: Spain's first village to hear the words 'historic monument' spoken in its streets
places_taglines.ES::miranda-del-castanar: Where the entire medieval wall still stands guard after seven centuries
places_taglines.ES::puente-del-congosto: Thirteen arches crossing the Tormes, built to collect tolls and defend a kingdom
places_taglines.ES::alba-de-tormes: Where Saint Teresa's heart rests in silver and dukes once ruled Castile
places_taglines.ES::monleon: Fewer than a hundred souls behind medieval walls that guidebooks forgot
places_taglines.ES::ledesma: A granite cliff above the Tormes where Romans and kings both chose to settle
places_taglines.ES::san-felices-de-los-gallegos: Built by Portuguese kings, seized by Castilian armies, perched above a 200-meter gorge
places_taglines.ES::salamanca: Golden stone and ancient learning where the Tormes meets the Via de la Plata

highlights: La Alberca, Spain's first protected historic-artistic site (1940), with its serrana architecture in granite and timber
Miranda del Castañar, one of only two Spanish towns with its entire medieval wall circuit intact (631 m)
Mogarraz and its 800 portraits painted by Florencio Maíllo since 2012 on every house façade
San Felices de los Gallegos and the spectacular Arribes del Duero gorges, 200-meter canyons on the Portuguese border

itineraries.title: Secret Extremadura: From Plasencia to Zafra Through Valleys and Dehesas
itineraries.subtitle: An 8-day slow travel road trip through the two hidden halves of Extremadura, weaving between cherry orchards in bloom, medieval Jewish quarters, an imperial monastery, Roman ramparts, and Portuguese Manueline architecture.
itineraries.notes: Monfragüe, Cáceres, and Mérida are treated as transit stops—if you haven't visited them yet, add an extra day for each. The cherry blossom in the Valle del Jerte typically happens late March through early April (about 15 days). The Garganta de los Infiernos requires authorized 4x4 access. Granadilla is closed on Mondays.

visits.text: Plasencia keeps its treasures close to the chest. Founded in 1186 by King Alfonso VIII of Castile, it boasts a unique quirk in Spain: two cathedrals stuck side by side. The Catedral Vieja, begun in the 13th century, is a fine example of Romanesque-Gothic transition—sober, compact, with a baroque altarpiece and a stunning ivory Christ. The Catedral Nueva, started in the 15th century, was never finished—but what exists is breathtaking: soaring ribbed vaults, a magnificent altarpiece attributed to Gregorio Fernández, and a heady mix of flamboyant Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque. They're visited together—don't choose, see both.

The Plaza Mayor of Plasencia has been the beating heart of the city since medieval times. Irregularly shaped and ringed by soportales (arcades) lined with bars and restaurants, it hosts a market every Tuesday that's been running uninterrupted since the 12th century. Grab a terrace seat and watch the unhurried rhythm of this 40,000-soul town that carries itself like a regional capital.

Plasencia is part of the Red de Juderías de España—the network of Spain's historic Jewish quarters. The Jewish community settled here from the city's founding in 1186. Traces survive in two neighborhoods: the old judería de la Mota (around today's Parador, a former San Vicente Ferrer convent built on the site of the first synagogue confiscated in 1477) and the "new judería" around Calle Trujillo and Calle Zapatería. In these alleys, plaques set into the pavement bear the names of Jewish families who lived here—Yuçe Alaçán, Abraham Almale, Samuel... Don't miss the Jewish cemetery of El Berrocal outside the walls: it's the only preserved Jewish burial ground in Extremadura, with about twenty anthropomorphic tombs carved into rock, facing Jerusalem.

Walk the circuit of Plasencia's medieval ramparts, still well-preserved along much of their length. You'll pass through the Trujillo, Coria, and Berrozana gates and cross paths with the acueducto—a restored medieval aqueduct running through the San Antón neighborhood. The Palacio del Marqués de Mirabel, built on the site of the old judería de la Mota in the 15th century, is worth a look for its facade and garden (visible from the street).

activities.text: Northern Extremadura's cuisine is generous and underrated. Try Torta del Casar—a creamy soft-paste cheese eaten with a spoon—migas extremeñas (fried breadcrumbs with chorizo and pimiento), jamón ibérico de bellota, and Valle del Jerte cherries in season. The Plaza Mayor and surrounding streets are packed with tapas bars where you'll eat well and abundantly.

practical_info.tip: Tuesday, market day, is the best time to discover local products in the Plaza Mayor

map_keywords: Garganta de los Infiernos Valle del Jerte

visits.text: Devote the morning to the Valle del Jerte, one of Extremadura's most spectacular landscapes. The name comes from Arabic "xerete"—crystalline river—and that's exactly what it is: a deep V-shaped valley framed by the Sierra de Tormantos and the Traslasierra mountains, where dozens of gargantas (gorges) run with mountain water. The valley holds 1.5 million cherry trees whose simultaneous flowering, usually late March to early April, transforms the slopes into a vast white mantle—this is the Cerezo en Flor festival, declared a National Tourist Interest celebration. In summer, the gargantas become natural swimming spots of rare beauty.

The valley's jewel is the Reserva Natural de la Garganta de los Infiernos, 7,000 protected hectares since 1994—Extremadura's second most-visited natural area. The circular trail (about 7 km, 2 hours) starts from the Interpretation Centre and descends to Los Pilones—giant's kettles carved into granite by water over centuries, forming natural pools of crystalline water where you can swim in summer. The path threads through oak, chestnut, and cherry forests, with waterfalls at every turn. Motor access is forbidden—tour operators offer trips in authorized 4x4s to the trailhead.

Heading down the valley, stop at Cabezuela del Valle, whose historic center is classified as a Conjunto Histórico-Artístico. Its narrow streets with half-timbered chestnut houses, overhanging balconies, and heraldic doorways make it one of the valley's prettiest villages. The church's detached tower and the Plaza de la Independencia deserve a photo stop.

In the afternoon, head to Hervás in the neighboring Valle del Ambroz. This village has one of Spain's best-preserved juderías—classified as Conjunto Histórico-Artístico since 1969. The Jewish community settled here after the 1391 pogroms, welcomed by the Duke of Béjar. At its peak in the 15th century, nearly 50 Jewish families lived here. Walk down Calle Abajo, the main street of the barrio judío: the adobe and timber-framed chestnut houses, streets so narrow you could touch both walls with outstretched arms (Travesía del Moral is 55 cm wide—one of Spain's narrowest), Stars of David carved on facades, and street names like "Amistad Judeo-Cristiana" (Jewish-Christian Friendship) all testify to a past that remains very present.

Finish at the Puente de la Fuente Chiquita, a sturdy medieval granite bridge spanning the Río Ambroz at the foot of the judería. It's one of Hervás's most photographed images, especially in autumn when the chestnuts turn golden. At number 19 Calle Rabilero, a building wider than the others occupies the site of the old synagogue. In July, the Fiesta de los Conversos reenacts medieval life and the 1492 expulsion in an open-air performance by the river.

activities.text: Hervás and Valle del Ambroz cuisine centers on the chestnut—which provides timber for houses, autumn fruit, and accompaniment to many dishes. In autumn (late October through December), the Otoñada celebrates chestnuts with markets, tastings, and walks through centuries-old chestnut groves. The rest of the year, try dulces sefardíes (Sephardic-inspired pastries) that some Hervás restaurants offer on themed menus.

practical_info.tip: The Hospedería Valle del Ambroz (former 17th-century convent converted to a 4-star hotel) is a great place to stay in Hervás

map_keywords: Monasterio de Yuste Cuacos de Yuste

visits.text: Start the day at the Monastery of Yuste, a European Heritage site nestled in the Sierra de Gredos foothills near Cuacos de Yuste. This is where Charles V—Charles I of Spain and V of the Holy Roman Empire, the most powerful man in the world—chose to retire after abdicating in 1556 in favor of his son Philip II. He arrived on February 3, 1557, with an entourage of about sixty people. His chamber was positioned against the church choir so he could attend mass from his bed—he suffered from terrible gout that confined him to his litter. He died here on September 21, 1558, at age 58, from malarial fevers. The visit shows both cloisters (one Gothic, one Renaissance), the 15th-century church, and the emperor's apartments—surprisingly austere for the man who'd ruled an empire where the sun never set.

Head to Coria in the afternoon (50 km, 45 minutes). This town is one of Spain's oldest—the ancient Caurium of the Vettones, which became a Roman municipium. Its Roman walls, built between the 3rd and 4th centuries, are probably the best-preserved as a whole set in all of Europe: over 1,100 meters of ramparts with about twenty square towers and four gates, two of them original Roman (Puerta del Sol and Puerta de la Guía). The walls are 8 meters thick in places. Walk the circuit—it's about a 30-minute stroll that gives you vertigo when you think these stones have been standing for 1,700 years.

Coria's cathedral, built between the 15th and 18th centuries on the remains of a Visigothic church, blends Gothic, Plateresque, and Baroque styles. Its Plateresque facade is remarkable, as is its Churrigueresque tower offering superb views over town and countryside. The cathedral guards an extraordinary relic: what's claimed to be the tablecloth from Christ's Last Supper, attested in a papal bull from 1404. Admission is free and opening hours are generous (closed Monday mornings).

Don't miss the bridge without a river—Coria's most surreal image. This five-arched stone bridge, built in the 16th century probably on earlier Roman foundations, spanned the Río Alagón. But the river changed course—likely gradually following floods, worsened by the 1755 Lisbon earthquake—and now flows several hundred meters away. Result: a bridge without a river and a river without a bridge. Walk under the arches for the best photo, especially late afternoon.

If daylight permits, detour 30 minutes to Granadilla, a fascinating ghost village. This fortified villa of Almohad origin (9th century), which governed 15 municipalities under its jurisdiction in the Middle Ages, was emptied of inhabitants in 1955 when Franco's regime decided to build the Gabriel y Galán dam. Cruel irony: the water never reached the village. Declared a Conjunto Histórico-Artístico in 1980, it was partially restored through an educational program. You can visit the cobbled lanes, the circular wall (one of Spain's best-preserved alongside Lugo and Ávila), and the 15th-century castle built by the Duke of Alba, whose terrace offers striking views over the reservoir and hills.

activities.text: In the evening in Coria, try dulces de las monjas—cloistered convent sweets. The town's convents sell handmade pastries (quemados, dulces de yema) through a rotating hatch, without seeing the nuns who make them. It's a typically Spanish tradition that persists in Extremadura's small towns.

practical_info.tip: The convent is near the Plaza Mayor. Ring the bell and wait—the nuns aren't in a hurry

visits.text: En route south, cross through Monfragüe National Park, one of Europe's top raptor-watching sites. Stop at the Salto del Gitano viewpoint: from this lookout above the Tajo River, you'll observe—almost guaranteed—griffon vultures gliding above their cliff-nesting colonies, black storks, Spanish imperial eagles, and sometimes black vultures. During breeding season (February–June), the spectacle is guaranteed. Allow 1 hour for the viewpoint, more if you climb to Monfragüe castle for 360° views over the dehesas.

Arrive in Cáceres late afternoon. If you haven't yet seen the Ciudad Monumental (UNESCO since 1986), treat yourself to at least a twilight stroll through its lanes—when day-trippers have left and the golden-stone Renaissance palaces light up. Plaza de Santa María, the Bujaco tower, and the Palacio de los Golfines de Abajo deserve a look even in passing. If you've already visited Cáceres, simply enjoy the evening on a Plaza Mayor terrace.

activities.text: Cáceres is a tapas town. The bars around Plaza Mayor and Calle Pintores serve generous portions of jamón ibérico, torta del casar, and pimientos asados. Order a vino de pitarra—an artisanal local wine you'll hardly find outside Extremadura.

practical_info.tip: The bars around Plaza Mayor are liveliest in the evening, especially on weekends

visits.text: Mérida is the ancient Augusta Emerita, capital of the Roman province of Lusitania founded in 25 BC, and one of the best-preserved Roman sites outside Italy (UNESCO since 1993). If you haven't visited yet, absolutely don't miss the Roman theatre—built between 16 and 15 BC, it still hosts a classical theatre festival every summer. Its scaenae frons with Corinthian columns, 17 meters high, is one of the most spectacular in the Roman Empire. Right next door, the amphitheatre (8 BC, 15,000 seats) completes the picture.

Cross the Puente Romano over the Guadiana—792 meters long, 60 arches, one of the longest Roman bridges still standing. The crossing is pedestrian and offers beautiful views over the city and Arab Alcazaba at twilight. If you've already seen Mérida, simply pass through town and enjoy the evening dining near the Temple of Diana.

activities.text: Mérida's cuisine is that of the dehesa—the oak-pasture landscape covering much of Extremadura. Try Iberian pork in all its forms (lomo, presa, secreto), caldereta de cordero (lamb stew), and wines from Tierra de Barros, the wine zone extending south toward Almendralejo.

practical_info.tip: Restaurants around the Temple of Diana and Plaza de España are best situated

map_keywords: Alcázar de los Duques de Feria Zafra

visits.text: Zafra is nicknamed "little Seville" for its white streets, flower-filled patios, and Andalusian atmosphere—but it's stayed far quieter than its big neighbor. Start with the Alcázar de los Duques de Feria, a 15th-century fortress flanked by nine crenellated towers, now converted to a Parador. Hernán Cortés stayed here before sailing off to conquer Mexico. Even if you're not spending the night, step inside to see the arcaded interior courtyard and coffered ceilings—Parador staff are used to curious visitors.

Zafra has two adjoining squares forming the town's heart. Plaza Grande, vast and airy, is lined with porticoes sheltering cafés and terraces. Plaza Chica, more intimate, was the medieval market site—the stone columns bordering it still bear the marks of standard measures carved by merchants to verify weights and lengths. The covered passage connecting the two squares is a charming detail.

The Colegiata de la Candelaria, from the 15th century, is Zafra's main church. Its main altarpiece is impressive and its position on Plaza Grande makes it a natural landmark. Just next door, the Convento de Santa Clara (15th century) shelters remarkable altarpieces and wrought-iron grilles.

Enter Zafra through the Puerta de Jerez, one of Extremadura's finest town gates. From there, wander the historic center's streets, declared a Bien de Interés Cultural—the whitewashed palaces, wrought-iron balconies, and patios glimpsed through half-open doors justify the town's nickname. In the evening, the atmosphere on Plaza Grande's terraces is delightful.

activities.text: Zafra sits at the heart of Tierra de Barros, Extremadura's main wine region. Tour operators offer bodega visits in the area, especially toward Almendralejo (30 km), the region's "wine capital." Extremadura wines, long underrated, have improved tremendously—try reds from tempranillo and garnacha, and whites from pardina and cayetana.

practical_info.tip: Booking recommended for bodega visits. Almendralejo is 20 minutes from Zafra

activities.text: If you prefer staying in town, Zafra hosts a lively flea market on Thursdays in Plaza Grande, a direct legacy of the medieval fairs that made the town prosperous. The Feria Internacional Ganadera (livestock fair) in September–October is one of Lower Extremadura's major events—if you're there then, don't miss it.

practical_info.tip: Thursday is the best day to visit Zafra—market plus old town animation

visits.text: Dedicate the day to Olivenza, 85 km northwest of Zafra. It's one of Spain's most unusual places: a Portuguese town on Spanish soil. Founded by the Templars in the 13th century, it remained Portuguese for five centuries (1297–1801) before being annexed by Spain during the brief War of the Oranges. Portugal has never recognized this annexation—officially, Olivenza is still claimed by Lisbon. Result: black-and-white Portuguese-style cobblestones, Manueline architecture, and azulejos coexist with Spanish signage. Since 2010, street names are posted in both languages.

The Igreja de Santa María Magdalena is Olivenza's masterpiece and the finest example of Manueline architecture outside Portugal—after Lisbon's Jerónimos Monastery. Commissioned by King Manuel I in 1510 and completed in 1560, it has three naves resting on eight twisted granite columns typical of Manueline style. The walls are covered with 18th-century blue-and-white azulejos, and six baroque altarpieces complete the ensemble. In 2012, Spanish internet users voted it "Spain's most beautiful corner"—a delicious paradox for an utterly Portuguese monument.

Olivenza's castle, built by the Templars then reinforced under Portuguese kings, has a keep tower 37 meters high—the tallest on the entire Spanish-Portuguese border. Erected in 1488 on flat terrain, it had to compensate through height what the land didn't provide in natural defense. It's accessed by a ramp (no stairs)—so it's accessible to all. From the top, the panorama sweeps across the plain to the Guadiana and Portugal. The castle also houses the Museo Etnográfico González Santana, one of Extremadura's richest ethnographic museums.

Stop by the Santa Casa de la Misericordia, founded in 1501 by King Manuel I himself. The chapel's interior walls are covered with 18th-century Portuguese azulejos depicting Old and New Testament scenes—a remarkable collection. The town hall on Plaza de la Constitución preserves a Manueline doorway that's the town symbol.

activities.text: Olivenza is a gastronomic border post: you'll eat migas extremeñas alongside bacalhau Portuguese-style, and convent pastries blend traditions from both countries. Lunch in the historic center and taste both cuisines—it's the only place in Spain where that's possible in the same village.

practical_info.tip: Olivenza is 85 km from Zafra (1h10 drive). Plan the whole day

map_keywords: Fortaleza Templaria Jerez de los Caballeros
Iglesia San Bartolomé Jerez de los Caballeros
Iglesia La Encarnación Jerez de los Caballeros
Torre Sangrienta Jerez de los Caballeros

visits.text: Jerez de los Caballeros is the final stop on this itinerary—and what a finale. Birthplace of Vasco Núñez de Balboa, the first European to sight the Pacific Ocean in 1513, it has a spectacular historic center dominated by the silhouettes of its baroque towers. The Templar fortress, built in the 13th century when the Knights Templar controlled this frontier with Muslim Andalusia, overlooks the town from a hilltop. It's in the Torre Sangrienta (Bloody Tower) that Jerez's last Templars were supposedly massacred in 1312 during the dissolution of the order. The legend is probably exaggerated, but it fits the mood of the place.

Jerez's two baroque churches are among Extremadura's finest. The Iglesia de San Bartolomé has a bell tower covered in colorful azulejos that recalls Seville's bell towers—it's the town's iconic image.
